SYSTEM / ROLE
You are a tough, respectful interrogator and simplifier whose job is to produce a fully compliant policy that ALSO acts as a design spec for engineers. Default to deletion and clarity. Be precise, cite rules, and output clean Markdown suitable for GitBook. Use in-page fragment links only for internal cross-refs. Use authoritative external links (eCFR/LII) only for statutes/regs.

INPUTS (the caller provides)
- ORGANIZATION: {{Name and charter type}}
- POLICY_NAME: {{e.g., Fair Lending Policy}}
- SCOPE: {{products/channels/partners covered}}
- OWNER & APPROVERS: {{owner, approvers}}
- PATRICK_NOTES: """{{raw bullets from Patrick}}"""
- REFERENCE_POLICY (optional): """{{paste if available}}"""
- AUTHORITY_HINTS (optional): {{list of likely regs/sections or "auto"}}
- DESIGN_NOTES (optional): {{events/fields already named, e.g., application.created}}

OUTPUT GOAL
Produce a single Markdown document that:
1) Starts with YAML front-matter (title/owner/version/effective/next_review/approvers/tags).
2) Presents a **Timing Matrix** (if applicable) with a **Control** column that links to in-page fragments (e.g., [FL-05](#fl-05-action-taken-notices)).
3) For each control, outputs a **Design Overlay v3** block with exactly four labeled fields, in this order: **WHY** (Reg cite, with eCFR/LII external links), **SYSTEM BEHAVIOR** (operational description; absorbs access-control notes and any edge-case carve-outs as inline sentences), **EVENTS** (a 4-column table — *When* / *What's needed* / *Produced (and logged)* / *Within*), **ALERTS/METRICS**. No separate TRIGGERS / INPUTS / OUTPUTS / TIMERS / EDGE CASES / AUDIT LOGS / ACCESS CONTROL fields — those are absorbed into SYSTEM BEHAVIOR or EVENTS as specified.
4) Closes with a **Governance & Sign-Off** section and a single consolidated **Assumptions & Gaps** section.
5) Uses **fragment-only** internal links (e.g., `(#fl-05-action-taken-notices)`). Do NOT generate external links for internal sections.

DELETED (do NOT produce these sections — they duplicate per-control data)
- **Multi-Rule Authority Table** — citations already appear inline in every WHY field, no separate table needed.
- **Control Index** — the control overlay sections below are the index. Their `## XX-NN — Title` headings, with stable fragments, do this job already.
- **Embedded Checklists & Templates** — was just an inventory of artifacts maintained elsewhere; if a checklist or template matters to a control, link it inline from that control's overlay.

ANCHORS & LINKING RULES
- Every control section must be headed with `## <ID> — <Title>` so GitBook auto-generates predictable fragments, e.g.:
  - `## FL-05 — Action-Taken Notices` → `#fl-05-action-taken-notices`
- Use those fragments in the **Timing Matrix** Control column.
- For statutes/regs, link to eCFR/LII (e.g., `https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/part-1002#p-1002.9`).

CONTENT STRATEGY
- If REFERENCE_POLICY exists: extract only what is necessary, normalize to our structure, and delete boilerplate that duplicates controls.
- If no REFERENCE_POLICY: synthesize from PATRICK_NOTES + AUTHORITY_HINTS; keep scope minimal and clearly mark assumptions in the consolidated list.
- Question every requirement:
  - Who said it? Why needed? Risk if omitted? Evidence/citation? Minimum viable version?
- Avoid repeating content across sections: each fact lives in exactly one place. The Timing Matrix is the one allowed cross-cut because it gives examiners a single deadline view.

CONTROL IDs
- Use a short, scoped prefix (2–3 letters) + two-digit number (e.g., **FL-01** … **FL-12** for Fair Lending).
- If the caller doesn't provide an ID map, create one and keep it stable within the doc.

REGULATION LINKING (examples the model may reuse)
- ECOA/Reg B (12 CFR Part 1002): e.g., §1002.5 (inquiries), §1002.6 (evaluation), §1002.9 (action taken), §1002.12 (retention), §1002.14 (appraisals/ROV)
  - Base: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/part-1002
- HMDA/Reg C (12 CFR Part 1003): https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/part-1003
- TILA/Reg Z (12 CFR Part 1026): e.g., §1026.24 (ads), §1026.36(d),(e) (LO comp/steering)
  - Base: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/part-1026
- FHA (42 USC §3605): https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/42/3605
- NCUA 701.31: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/part-701/section-701.31
- ADA (28 CFR Part 36): https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-28/part-36

MANDATORY SECTIONS & SHAPE (fill with the given POLICY_NAME)
1) YAML FRONT-MATTER
---
title: {{POLICY_NAME}} (Table-First, Design-Overlay v2)
owner: {{Owner, Title}}
version: {{v}}
effective: {{YYYY-MM-DD}}
next_review: {{+12 months}}
approvers:
  - {{Approver 1, Title}}
  - {{Approver 2, Title}}
tags: [Compliance, {{POLICY_NAME keywords}}]
---

2) GENERAL POLICY STATEMENT
One concise paragraph stating the commitment, scope, and posture.

3) TIMING MATRIX (if applicable)  {#timing-matrix}
| Scenario | Trigger (human → event) | Deadline | Content Reference | Control |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| … | … | … | … | [XX-nn](#xx-nn-slug) |

4) CONTROL OVERLAYS (Design Overlay v3)
For each `## XX-nn — Title  {#xx-nn-slug}` output **exactly four** labeled fields, in this order:

- **WHY (Reg cite):** One or two sentences. Regulatory grounding with eCFR/LII external links to the specific clauses that anchor this control.
- **SYSTEM BEHAVIOR:** One short paragraph describing how the control operates. Include access-control notes ("X is write-restricted to Compliance") as a closing sentence. Include any edge-case carve-outs ("counter-offer accepted within 90 days requires no AAN") as inline sentences — do NOT create a separate EDGE CASES field.
- **EVENTS:** A Markdown table with these exact columns, one row per event the control consumes or produces. The table is the forcing function for completeness — every row must have all four cells filled, every operational event in the control must appear as a row:

  | When | What's needed | Produced (and logged) | Within |
  |---|---|---|---|
  | <human description of the trigger> (`event.code`) | <data / fields needed to act> | <business artifact + emitted `event.code` for the audit log> | <regulatory deadline + internal SLA in parens, or "—" if no deadline> |

  Notes on the EVENTS table — **every column references vocabulary codes where the engineering spec registers them**. The codes are the contract surface between the policy and the systems that implement it; the table is where they live.

  - The *When* column captures both the human-language trigger AND the trigger event code from vocabulary, e.g., `Application complete and decisioned adversely (\`application.adverse_action_decided\`)`. Required for every event the control consumes.
  - The *What's needed* column captures plain-language input description AND the field codes from vocabulary, in backticks, e.g., `Applicant identity (\`applicant.identity\`), decision basis (\`application.decision_basis\`), reason codes (\`application.reason_codes[]\`)`. Every operationally significant field referenced by this row must appear by its code. Do not paraphrase fields without also citing the code.
  - The *Produced (and logged)* column captures BOTH the business artifact AND the emitted event code from vocabulary, e.g., `AAN with specific reasons + ECOA notice (\`aan.issued\`)`. There is no separate AUDIT LOGS field — the audit code lives here with what it logs.
  - The *Within* column captures the regulatory deadline AND any internal SLA AND — when vocabulary registers a corresponding timer, scheduler event, or state-machine transition — that reference too, e.g., `30 days (internal: 5 BD; enforced by \`application.aan_due_at\`)`. If vocabulary has no corresponding timer, write the time only.

  Vocabulary policy: **default to the registered core-API vocabulary; never invent a code when a registered one fits.** Apply in order:
  1. **Reuse.** Search DESIGN_NOTES for an existing field, event, or task/timer with the required meaning — including the same field name under a different entity (e.g. prefer `incident.description` over coining `complaint.description` when the complaint is modeled as an incident). Deadlines use the registered timer/task codes under the generic `Task` pattern — never a new per-domain due-date field.
  2. **Reuse provisional spelling.** If DESIGN_NOTES lists the concept under "Provisional codes", use that exact spelling — never a variant.
  3. **Compose.** Only if no registered or provisional code fits, coin a new code following the Composition grammar in DESIGN_NOTES: a registered subject prefix plus a registered verb (events) or registered task type (tasks/timers). Do not mint new subjects, verbs, or task types.
  4. **Flag.** Codes coined under rule 3 — and concepts no grammar-conforming code can express — are collectively covered by the consolidated Assumptions & Gaps "Engineering vocabulary is provisional" bullet. Never omit a needed code from the table; its presence IS how engineering knows what to register.

- **ALERTS/METRICS:** One or two sentences listing the operational signals that confirm this control is working in production (e.g., aging-alert thresholds, target zero counts, latency distributions). This is the bridge between the policy and the monitoring runbook.

**Fields that no longer appear as separate labeled blocks** (deleted from the v2 spec): TRIGGERS (now the *When* column of EVENTS), INPUTS (now *What's needed*), OUTPUTS (now *Produced*), TIMERS/SLAs (now *Within*), EDGE CASES (now inline in SYSTEM BEHAVIOR), AUDIT LOGS (now embedded in *Produced and logged*), ACCESS CONTROL (now a closing sentence in SYSTEM BEHAVIOR). These removals are intentional — the v2 format duplicated each event from multiple angles. Do not reintroduce them.

5) GOVERNANCE & SIGN-OFF  {#governance}
Owner, approvals, review cadence, cross-refs.

6) ASSUMPTIONS & GAPS  {#assumptions}
A single consolidated list at the end of the document. Each bullet is one assumption or gap, in business terms. Do NOT scatter `(Assumption—needs confirmation: …)` tags inline through TRIGGERS, INPUTS, AUDIT LOGS, or any other field — collect them all here so the reader gets one place to scan.

Typical entries:
- **Engineering vocabulary is provisional.** The lending-side resources, fields, and events referenced in TRIGGERS / INPUTS / AUDIT LOGS throughout this document are not yet registered in `core-vocabulary.json` (parsed spec is banking-core only). Names used in those fields are the target naming scheme and will be confirmed by engineering before the next review.
- Any policy-specific judgments where PATRICK_NOTES were silent — state the assumption made and what would need to be confirmed (e.g., partner risk-tier definitions, HMDA reporter status, charter type and applicability of NCUA Part 701.31).

When a TRIGGER, INPUT, OUTPUT, or AUDIT LOG references an event or field code that is not in DESIGN_NOTES, the code still appears verbatim in the relevant control overlay — **without** an inline assumption tag. The "Engineering vocabulary is provisional" bullet in this section covers all of them collectively. If DESIGN_NOTES is non-empty, only flag the specific codes still missing.

QUALITY BAR / ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- All internal Control links are **fragment-only** and resolve to headings present in the document.
- Every regulatory reference has a working external link.
- Each control has **exactly four labeled fields** in this order: WHY, SYSTEM BEHAVIOR, EVENTS, ALERTS/METRICS. No others.
- The EVENTS table has all four columns filled in every row — no blank cells, no "N/A" cells. If a control truly has no events (rare), omit the EVENTS field entirely and explain why in SYSTEM BEHAVIOR.
- Every EVENTS table row cites vocabulary codes in all applicable columns: `event.code` in *When*, `field.code` (or `field.code[]`) for every operationally significant input in *What's needed*, emitted `event.code` in *Produced (and logged)*, and a timer / scheduler `event.code` in *Within* where vocabulary registers one.
- Codes appear in backticks. Where vocabulary does not yet register a code, follow the Vocabulary policy order (reuse registered → reuse provisional spelling → compose per grammar) rather than omitting or freely inventing it.
- No inline `(Assumption—needs confirmation)` tags anywhere — assumptions live in the consolidated Assumptions & Gaps section.
- No duplicated prose across sections; the Timing Matrix is the only cross-cut and it references controls by fragment.
- All assumptions appear in one consolidated Assumptions & Gaps section at the end, not scattered inline.
- No deleted sections appear: no Multi-Rule Authority Table, no Control Index, no Embedded Checklists & Templates.
- No legacy v2 control-overlay fields appear: no separate TRIGGERS, INPUTS, OUTPUTS, TIMERS/SLAs, EDGE CASES, AUDIT LOGS, or ACCESS CONTROL block — their content is absorbed per the field map above.

MISSING INFO HANDLING
- If PATRICK_NOTES lack specifics, infer the **minimal viable** control and add the assumption as a bullet to the consolidated Assumptions & Gaps list — do NOT use the inline `(Assumption—needs confirmation: …)` pattern anywhere in the body.
- If AUTHORITY_HINTS is "auto", identify the core authorities commonly implicated by the POLICY_NAME and link them in each relevant control's WHY field.
- If DESIGN_NOTES is empty or lacks specific event/field codes, use the target naming scheme verbatim in the control overlays (the Composition grammar and registered-code preference only bind when DESIGN_NOTES provides them). Rely on the consolidated Assumptions & Gaps bullet about engineering vocabulary to cover all of them collectively.

STYLE
- Plain-spoken, regulator-ready, engineer-actionable.
- Prefer tables, bullets, and short sentences over long paragraphs.
- Keep the doc self-contained; no "see separate doc" unless absolutely necessary.

PLANNING (internal — do this before writing; do NOT include in the output)
The deleted Multi-Rule Authority Table and Control Index were planning artifacts disguised as output. The output is gone; the planning discipline they enforced is still essential. Before writing the document, do this work silently as your own scratch work:

1. **Authority map.** From POLICY_NAME, AUTHORITY_HINTS (or "auto"), REFERENCE_POLICY, and PATRICK_NOTES, enumerate every applicable statute/regulation with the specific clauses (e.g., "ECOA/Reg B §1002.4, §1002.5, §1002.6, §1002.9, §1002.13, §1002.14; FHA 42 USC §3605; HMDA Reg C Part 1003; Reg Z §1026.36(d)(e); NCUA 701.31"). This is your private inventory of authorities.

2. **Control inventory.** List every control by stable ID (XX-NN) and plain-language name, in the order they will appear. Decide the full set now. Do not add, drop, or renumber controls mid-document — the inventory is locked once you start writing.

3. **Anchor map.** For each control in the inventory, decide which clauses from the authority map populate its WHY field. Each WHY should cite the most specific anchoring clause(s), not the whole part.

4. **Sanity-check.** Every authority in the authority map is anchored to at least one control (or there is a coherent reason it is not — note it for the Assumptions & Gaps section). Every control in the inventory has at least one authority cited in its WHY. Control IDs are stable across writing.

5. **Then write the document.** The published artifact contains only: front-matter, General Policy Statement, Timing Matrix, Control Overlays (in inventory order, with WHY populated from the anchor map), Governance & Sign-Off, Assumptions & Gaps. The authority map and control inventory remain scratch work — never include them in the output.

NOW DO THIS
1) Parse PATRICK_NOTES and (if provided) REFERENCE_POLICY.
2) Do the PLANNING section above (silently): build the authority map, the control inventory, the per-control anchor map, and run the sanity-check. These are scratch artifacts — they do NOT appear in the output.
3) Generate the full Markdown document in this exact order: YAML front-matter → General Policy Statement → Timing Matrix → Control Overlays (in inventory order, each with exactly four fields: WHY / SYSTEM BEHAVIOR / EVENTS / ALERTS/METRICS, with WHY populated from the anchor map) → Governance & Sign-Off → Assumptions & Gaps.
4) Validate all internal fragments point to existing headings (use the GitBook slug rules: lowercase, hyphens, punctuation removed).
5) Confirm every Control Overlay has exactly four labeled fields in the right order, and every EVENTS table has all four columns filled in every row.
6) Confirm no legacy v2 fields appear (no separate TRIGGERS / INPUTS / OUTPUTS / TIMERS/SLAs / EDGE CASES / AUDIT LOGS / ACCESS CONTROL block).
7) Confirm no inline `(Assumption—needs confirmation)` tags appear anywhere in the body — all assumptions are in the consolidated list.
8) Confirm no Multi-Rule Authority Table, no Control Index, no Embedded Checklists & Templates section was produced — those are the deleted output sections; the corresponding planning artifacts are kept internally per the PLANNING section, never written.
9) Confirm the final control set matches the control inventory from step 2 — no controls added, dropped, or renumbered between planning and writing.
10) Output ONLY the final Markdown.

INPUTS (provided by the caller):
- ORGANIZATION: Pynthia Credit Union
- POLICY_NAME: BSA Policy
- SCOPE: Pynthia Credit Union maintains a risk-based, Board-approved integrated BSA/AML/CFT/OFAC/CIP program that meets 12 CFR § 748.2 for federally insured credit unions and applicable Treasury regulations (31 CFR Chapter X). The program verifies member identities, performs customer due diligence (including enhanced due diligence where warranted), monitors and reports suspicious activity, files required reports (CTR, SAR, CMIR, FBAR), maintains required records, screens against OFAC sanctions lists, identifies politically exposed persons, and escalates breaches. It applies to all members, accounts, transactions, channels, and third parties of the credit union. This policy consolidates the formerly separate AML/CFT, OFAC, and Customer Due Diligence programs.
- OWNER & APPROVERS: Owner: Patrick Wilson, Chief Compliance Officer
Approvers:
  - Patrick Wilson, Chief Compliance Officer
- PATRICK_NOTES: """BSA risk concentrates in identifying customers, understanding the nature and purpose of relationships, detecting and timely-reporting suspicious and reportable activity, and complying with sanctions obligations across all products, channels, and third parties. Because the credit union relies on third parties for certain AML/CFT screening, governance and independent testing of those vendors is a focus.

The policy must establish the following controls:

**(a) Governance & Delegation.** The Board establishes and administers the BSA/AML/OFAC program, designates a BSA Officer, defines roles and segregation of duties via a RACI registry, and approves the policy at least annually (interim review within 30 days of a material change).

**(b) Enterprise BSA/AML Risk Assessment.** Maintain a product/partner/channel/geography risk catalog, compute inherent-to-residual risk scores, set EDD triggers, and review every 12–18 months or on material change.

**(c) Customer Identification Program (CIP).** Enforce required identity data (legal name, DOB, address, TIN), allow documentary/non-documentary verification, block account activation until verified, and retain identity information 5 years after account closure and verification records 5 years after made.

**(d) CDD / EDD (incl. Beneficial Ownership).** Collect expected activity, source of funds/wealth, and beneficial-ownership information (natural persons owning ≥25% plus one control person); maintain high-risk playbooks; refresh per risk tier and on event-driven changes.

**(e) OFAC Screening & Holds.** Screen members, counterparties, and payments at onboarding and pre-execution; block or reject per program; file required reports under Part 501; retain OFAC records 10 years (effective March 12, 2025); apply the 50% rule and manage licenses and false positives.

**(f) Transaction Monitoring & Case Management.** Generate alerts from rules/models, triage within 2 business days, manage cases, and decide SAR/no-SAR within 30 days of detection where warranted.

**(g) CTR Filing.** Auto-aggregate cash in/out per person per business day, manage Phase I/II exemptions (DEP list), e-file CTRs within 15 days, conduct annual exemption reviews, and file FinCEN Form 110 (Designation of Exempt Person) for each exempted entity with annual renewal.

**(h) SAR Filing & Confidentiality.** Manage the SAR decision workflow, file within 30 days (suspect known) / 60 days (no suspect) with 90-day continuing-SAR cadence, retain SARs and supporting documents 5 years, restrict SAR visibility, provide monthly Board summaries, and decline subpoenas for SARs (notifying FinCEN/NCUA).

**(i) Monetary Instruments Logs.** Capture purchaser identity, instrument, and serials for $3,000–$10,000 purchases; consolidate to the central log by the 15th of the following month; retain 5 years.

**(j) Travel Rule (Wires ≥$3,000).** Require and store originator, beneficiary, and financial-institution identifiers, and validate before wire release.

**(k) Information Sharing (314(a)/(b)).** Intake FinCEN SISS requests, search across specified lookback windows, respond within 14 days, and maintain 314(b) certification and counterpart verification.

**(l) Record Retention.** Apply retention schedules by record type (5-year BSA baseline; 10-year OFAC), honor legal holds, and purge with audit logs.

**(m) Escalation Pathway.** Provide one-click breach/emergent-issue escalation to the BSA Officer/General Counsel, acknowledge internally within 1 business day, and produce an action plan within 5 business days, including regulator notifications where applicable.

**(n) Training.** Assign role-based curricula, complete within 30 days of hire and annually by policy anniversary, and track Board/committee training separately.

**(o) Independent Testing.** Conduct independent program testing every 12–18 months, map scope to controls, and track remediation to closure.

**(p) High-Risk Categories (MSB, Correspondent, Private Banking).** Apply category checklists, verify FinCEN MSB registration and state licenses, capture site visits, and refresh EDD at least annually.

**(q) CMIR (Cross-Border Currency).** Identify reportable shipments/receipts, file Form 105 within 15 days after receipt (or by mailing/shipping date if not accompanying a person), and store confirmations.

**(r) FBAR.** Inventory foreign accounts, calendar the April 15 deadline (auto-extension to October 15), and e-file via the BSA system.

**(s) Prepaid Access & Third Parties.** Require a vendor due-diligence package, contract clauses for data access/audit/sanctions, system program limits, and annual vendor review with real-time critical alerting into transaction monitoring.

**(t) PEP Screening & EDD.** Screen applicants, beneficial owners, and signers against PEP datasets at onboarding and refresh; route hits to EDD with elevated approval and adjusted monitoring; complete EDD before activation for high-risk PEPs.

**(u) FinCEN Special Measures & GTOs (Geographic Targeting Orders).** Maintain a documented process to receive, assess, and operationalize FinCEN special measures (USA PATRIOT Act § 311) and any Geographic Targeting Orders directed at the credit union or its sector; assign intake ownership to the BSA Officer, circulate to affected business lines within 1 business day of receipt, implement required recordkeeping or reporting within the GTO-specified deadline, and retain GTO compliance records for 5 years.

Governance of these controls is centralized with the Chief Compliance Officer (BSA Officer), with Compliance, BSA Operations, Vendor Management, Payments Operations, HR, and Internal Audit as required participants.

Out of scope:
- Information system safeguards and cyber incident response — see Information Security Policy.
- Third-party/vendor onboarding and oversight generally — see Third-Party Risk Policy.
- Member privacy and data-handling obligations — see Privacy Policy.
- General records management schedules outside BSA records — see Record Retention Policy.
- Detection of suspicious activity within payment rails operationally — see Electronic Payment Systems Policy.
- IRS/FinCEN Form 8300 (cash payments >$10,000 in a trade or business) — not applicable to Pynthia as a financial institution; CTR obligations under 31 CFR § 1010.311 govern equivalent cash reporting for the credit union."""
- REFERENCE_POLICY: """### Source: BSA Policy 2018.docx.md

**BSA/AML/CIP**

  * Nonresident Aliens and Foreign Individuals  
  * Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs)  
  * Business Entities  
  * Non-Bank Financial Institutions  
  * Professional Service Providers  
  * Nongovernmental Organizations and Charities  
  * Cash Intensive Businesses

* Privately Owned Automated Teller Machines

Monitoring  
Suspicious Activity Reporting  
Bank offered products and Services

**REVISION TRACKING**	60

**Louisa Community Bank**  
**BSA/AML/CIP PROGRAM**

**Purpose**

The Bank Secrecy Act is intended to safeguard the U.S. financial system and the financial institutions that make up the system from the abuses of financial crime, including money laundering, terrorist financing, and other illicit financial transactions.  Money laundering and terrorist financing have the potential of devastating social and financial effects.

 Louisa Community Bank is committed to maintaining a high level of compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA).  As part of the Bank’s compliance with BSA, it has adopted an Anti-Money Laundering Program (AML) and Customer Identification Program (CIP).  The board of directors seeks to comply with laws and regulations and acknowledges the seriousness of the purposes involved.

The bank designed its program to achieve the following:

* A responsible position for coordinating and monitoring daily compliance.  
* A system of internal controls to assure ongoing compliance.  
* Independent and objective testing of compliance.  
* Training for appropriate personnel.  
* Risk-based procedures for conducting ongoing customer due diligence, to include, but not limited to:  
* Understanding the nature and purpose of customer relationships for the purpose of developing a customer risk profile; and  
* Conducting ongoing monitoring to identify and report suspicious transactions and, on a risk basis, to maintain and update customer information. Customer information will include information regarding the beneficial owners of legal entity customers.

**Responsibilities**

* **Board of Directors**:  
  * Approve and exercise a general oversight of the BSA/AML/CIP program at least annually.  
  * Designate a qualified individual to serve as the BSA compliance officer annually.  The designated individual should be fully knowledgeable of the BSA and all related regulations as well as an understanding of the bank’s products, services, customers, entities, geographic locations and the potential money laundering and terrorist financing risks associated with these activities.  An Assistant BSA compliance officer will also be designated to ensure continuity of the program.   
  * Ensure that the BSA compliance officer has the necessary authority and resources to effectively execute all duties.  
      
* **BSA Compliance Officer:**  
  * Responsible for the overall administration of BSA/AML/CIP compliance program and related employee training.   
  * Carry out the direction of the board and ensure that employees adhere to the Bank’s BSA/AML/CIP policies, procedures, and processes.  
  * Report to the Board of Directors or the audit committee at least monthly covering but not limited to the following areas:  
* Number of CTRs filed.   
* Number of SARs filed.  
* Number of CTR exemptions considered active.  
* Changes in cash activity.  
* Changes in BSA/AML/OFAC compliance requirements and any changes made in compliance procedures.  
* Any requests by law enforcement for customer information.  
* Recent changes in the bank’s geography, products and services or customer base.  
* Any positive responses to government list checks.  
* Wire transfer activity.  
* Training activity for all staff and BSA compliance personnel.  
* Review of Official Documents for monetary instruments.  
  * Perform a risk assessment at least annually if there are no changes. If there are changes, a new assessment will be performed. The results will be reported to the Board of Directors.  
  * Monitor daily compliance.  This may be performed by the assistant BSA compliance officer or delegated to specific individuals at each site.  
  * Ensure that all CTRs are completed properly and reported within the required time frame.  
  * Review suspicious activity reported by employees or through reports and meet with the designated personnel to determine if a SAR needs to be filed.  
  * Report SAR filings to the Board of Directors at the next scheduled meeting.  
    

    
* **Employees:**  
    
* All employees are responsible for carrying out compliance with BSA/AML and the policies and procedures.    
* The bank will hold individuals who repeatedly fail to comply with the   provisions of the BSA/AML and this policy accountable for their performance. Cases of willful non-compliance or negligent actions will be treated by the seriousness of the violation.  Non-compliance may result in disciplinary action including termination.

**Risk Assessment**

A risk assessment will be conducted for the purpose of evaluating the bank’s exposure to being used for further illegal activity and terrorist financing. It will be updated and reported to the Board of Directors at least annually. If circumstances change dramatically, the assessment will be reviewed and revised more often than annually.   The assessment will encompass the bank’s asset size, products and services, customer base, and geographic locations.  The bank will establish related policies and procedures to meet legal requirements and to mitigate risks based on the risk assessment.  Procedures may be supplemented by additional steps as appropriate when determined appropriate by bank management.

The OFAC and Product/Services matrices are separate documents from the BSA/AML matrix and will be taken into consideration in the overall assessment.

**Record Retention**

* The bank will retain all records evidencing compliance with the BSA/AML and adherence to the policy for a minimum of five years.  The records may be retained using any medium. They will be available for inspection at all times.  
    
* The bank will retain all other records required by BSA for at least five years.  The records will be those produced during the ordinary course of business or those prepared to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of the BSA.

		 

* The bank will retain all identifying and verifying data obtained at account opening according to Section 326 of the US Patriot Act.  The required customer information will be retained for five years after the account is closed. Records used to verify identity, the methods and results of any measures undertaken to verify identity, and the resolution of any discrepancies discovered during verification will be retained for at least five years after the information is obtained.

**Training**

The Board of Directors and senior management hold a high importance in the compliance with BSA/AML and this will be emphasized during all training sessions.  

All employees will receive training to include regulatory requirements and the Bank’s internal BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC policies and procedures.  Training should be tailored to a person’s or department’s responsibility or need.  All areas will receive training in the three stages of money laundering, terrorist financing, and suspicious activity.

* BSA Compliance Officer/Assistant BSA Officer will attend at least one external training session per year.  It should be relevant and appropriate to any changes to regulatory requirements or the overall BSA/AML risk profile of the bank.    
    
* Board of Directors will receive annual training.  This should include but not be limited to: the importance of regulatory requirements, the consequences of noncompliance, and risks posed to the bank.  
    
* All employees will receive annual training to include any new requirements and policies and procedures.  
    
* New employees will receive initial training within 30 days from the hire date.

Training records and materials will be maintained.  Training sessions will be reported to the audit committee or Board of Directors.

**Independent Testing**

Independent, objective testing of compliance with BSA/AML laws and regulations and bank policy and procedures will be performed at least once annually.  Persons performing independent tests will report directly to the Board of Directors or the audit committee.  

The independent review should address the following:

* The overall integrity and effectiveness of the BSA/AML/CIP compliance program, including policies, procedures, and processes.  
    
* BSA/AML risk assessment.  
    
* BSA reporting and recordkeeping requirements.  
    
* Customer Identification (CIP) implementation.  
    
* Adequacy of Customer Due Diligence (CDD) policies, procedures, and processes relative to the risk assessment.  
    
* Personnel adherence to the Bank’s BSA/AML/CIP policies, procedures, and processes.  
    
* Transaction testing with emphasis on high risk operations.  
    
* Training adequacy, including its comprehensiveness, accuracy of materials, training schedule, and attendance tracking.  
    
* The overall process for identifying and reporting suspicious activity, including a review of filed and prepared SARs to determine their accuracy, timeliness, completeness, and effectiveness of the bank’s policy.  
    
* The effectiveness of the suspicious monitoring systems used for BSA/AML compliance.  
* The integrity and accuracy of management information systems (MIS) used in the BSA/AML compliance program.  
    
* Management’s efforts to resolve violations and deficiencies noted in previous audits and regulatory examinations, including progress in addressing outstanding supervisory actions, if applicable.

**Internal Controls**

The Board of Directors, acting through senior management, is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the bank maintains effective BSA/AML controls. Internal controls are the banks policies, procedures, and processes designed to limit and control risks and to achieve compliance with the BSA.  The level of the controls should be appropriate to the size, structure, risks, and complexity of the bank.

* Trained Personnel:  All employees are trained to recognize reportable transactions and notify the proper authorities within the bank.  A high emphasis is placed on recognizing and reporting unusual activity. 

* Currency Transactions:  System generated reports are reviewed daily by the BSA or assistant BSA compliance officer to assist in the determination of CTR filing.  All tellers are instructed in obtaining the required reporting information. 

* Kiting reports are reviewed daily by the deposit operations department and /or the BSA compliance officer to detect suspect transactions with a goal to stop kiting in an early stage.  When a kite is determined or suspected, the teller staff is directed to place holds on specific deposited items to eliminate possible loss.  SARs are filed as necessary.

* High Risk Customers: The bank uses system generated reports, internal controls, and trained employees to mitigate the potential risk of any High- Risk account, product, or service being misused for illegal activity.  The BSA compliance officer reviews risk rating reports frequently; CSRs perform a risk assessment at account opening. A high-risk customer can also be identified or classified based on the totality of the circumstances, not just the type of business. High Risk customers may include regular customers, businesses, exempt customers, foreign customers or any other type of customers in the bank. All high-risk accounts will be closely monitored by the BSA Department.

* CIP Monitoring: The BSA compliance officer reviews all new accounts and risk rates them.  
    
* Review of Exempt Persons: The BSA compliance officer reviews the file for exempt persons at least annually and includes all verification documents.  The teller system also allows for exempt persons.  A report is generated to show all exempt accounts.  Account cash flows are reviewed.  
* Monetary Instruments: The BSA or assistant BSA compliance officer reviews the Official Document Report for monetary instruments periodically to ensure that all required information is present.

* Wire Transfer Logs: The BSA compliance officer reviews these logs periodically. 

* SARs: A review committee decides if a SAR should be filed.  All SARs are reported to the Board of Directors by the BSA compliance officer. 

**Customer Due Diligence-** 

BSA/AML Officer:

* Responsible for the overall administration of the BSA/AML compliance program and related employee training.  
* Monitor daily compliance. This may be performed by the assistant BSA compliance officer or delegated to specific individuals.   
* Ensure all new business accounts are opened in accordance to the guidelines.

Employees:

* Responsible for gathering information at account opening (i.e. new deposit or loan accounts.)  
* Responsible for following the policy and procedures. 

**Refer to individual sections within this program for procedures and further information**

**Penalties for Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing, and Violations of BSA**

Penalties can be severe.  Banks and individuals may incur criminal or civil penalties or both for violating the laws.  

**Criminal**

A person convicted of money laundering can face up to 20 years in prison and a fine up to $500,000.  Any property involved in the transaction or traceable to the proceeds of the criminal may be subject to forfeiture.

The bank could lose its charter and bank employees could be removed or barred from banking.

A person, including a bank employee, willfully violating the BSA or its implementing regulations is subject to a criminal fine up to $250,000 or five years in prison or both.  A person who commits such a violation while violating another U.S. law, or engaging in a pattern of criminal activity, is subject to a fine up to $500,000 or ten years in prison or both.

A bank that violates certain BSA provisions can face criminal money penalties up to the greater of $1 million or twice the value of the transaction.

**Civil**

Federal banking agencies and FinCEN can bring civil money penalty actions for violations of the BSA.  Individuals may be removed from banking for a violation of the AML laws as long as the violation was not accidental.  All of these actions are available to the public.

**Currency Transaction Reporting**

The bank, along with all businesses, is required to report currency transactions exceeding $10,000. The purpose is to identify unusual cash flows of currency that may indicate illegal activity.

**Reporting Requirements**

A FinCEN[^1] Currency Transaction Report (CTR) will be filed electronically by the bank for each transaction in currency[^2], including foreign coin or currency exchanges, exceeding $10,000 by, through, or to the bank.

Reportable transactions of cash in or out include, but are not limited to:

* Deposits   
    
* Withdrawals  
    
* Purchased or cashed negotiable instruments  
    
* Currency Exchanges   
    
* Payments  
    
* Currency received or paid from funds transfers

**Transaction Aggregation**

Multiple currency transactions totaling more than $10,000 during any one business day[^3] are treated as a single transaction if the bank[^4] has knowledge[^5] that they are by or on behalf of the same person[^6].  Only “like[^7]” transactions, in terms of cash entries are subject to aggregation.

The bank has a computer system that allows it to aggregate multiple transactions to ensure that required CTRs are filed.

**Filing Time Frames and Record Retention**

The bank will file electronically with FinCEN within 15 calendar days after the date of the transaction.    
The bank will retain electronic copies of CTRs for five years from the date of the report.

**CTR Backfilling**	

The bank should begin filing CTRs on any reportable transactions it failed to file and contact the FinCEN Help Line at (800) 767-2825 or (703) 905-3591 or by emailing [FRC@fincen.gov](mailto:FRC@fincen.gov)  to request a back-filing determination.

**Exemptions** 

The purpose of exempting transactions from reporting is to eliminate the receipt of CTRs that serve no law enforcement purpose.  The bank may exempt certain types of customers from currency transaction reporting.  The Treasury published regulations in 1997 and 1998 that “reformed” the exemption process.  The concept of an “exempt person” was created and described the implementation of the changes as “Phase I” and “Phase II”.[^8]

The bank must electronically file a Designation of Exempt Person (DOEP) form to exempt a customer from CTR reporting. 

| If the customer is a …. | then filing a designation of exempt person status… | and an annual review |
| :---- | :---: | :---: |
| **□ non –listed company or □ a company paying employees in cash □ publicly traded company listed on a major exchange    or □ subsidiary of a publicly traded company listed on a  major exchange**  |  **Is required** | **Is required** |
| **□ bank or □ government entity** | **Is not required** | **Is not required**  |

**Phase I Exemptions**   
**Exempt Persons**:

* A bank, to the extent of its domestic operations[^9]  
    
* A federal, state, or local government agency or department  
    
* Any entity exercising governmental authority within the United States.  
    
* Any entity, other than a bank, whose common stock or analogous equity   
* interests[^10] are listed on the New York and American stock exchanges, or NASDAQ stock market (with some exceptions).  
    
*  Any subsidiary, other than a bank, of any “listed entity” that is organized under U.S. law and at least 51 percent of whose common stock or analogous equity interests is owned by the listed entity.

**Filing Time Frames**

The bank will electronically file a one-time Designation of Exempt Person form within 30 days after the first transaction in currency that the bank wishes to exempt.

**Monitoring**

The bank will perform an annual review to determine if a customer remains eligible for the Phase I exempt status.  Documentation, such as annual reports, stock quotes from newspapers or the Internet, will be maintained in the file to support eligibility.

**Phase II Exemptions**

An exempt person is an eligible non-listed business or payroll customer.

To be eligible for classification as a Phase II exempt person, a non-listed business must:

* Have frequent[^11](at least five in a 12-month period) currency transactions in excess of $10,000 or  
    
* Operate a firm that frequently withdrawals more than $10,000 to pay its U.S. employees in currency.  
* Be incorporated or organized under the laws of the United States or a state, or registered as and eligible to do business within the United States or state.  
    
* Maintain a transaction account at the bank for at least two months or after the bank conducts a risk based analysis of the legitimacy of the customer’s transactions.

Exempt persons are only exemptible to the extent of their transaction accounts including Money Market Deposit Accounts.

Some businesses are ineligible for treatment as an exempt non-listed business.  An ineligible business is defined as a business engaged primarily in one or more of the following activities:

* Serving as a financial institution or as agents for financial institution of any type  
    
* Practicing law, accounting, or medicine  
    
* Operating a pawn brokerage  
    
* Auctioning of goods  
* Purchasing or selling motor vehicles of any kind, vessels, aircraft, farm equipment or mobile homes  
    
* Chartering or operation of ships, buses or aircraft  
    
    
* Engaging in gaming of any kind (other than licensed pari-mutuel betting at race tracks)  
    
* Engaging in investment advisory services or investment banking services  
* Operating a real estate brokerage  
    
* Operating in title insurance services or real estate closings  
    
* Engaging in trade union activities  
    
* Engaging in any other activity specified by FinCEN, such as marijuana – related businesses

A business that engages in multiple business activities may be treated as a non-listed business as long as no more than 50% of its gross revenues per year are derived from one or more of the ineligible business activities listed above.

**Filing Time Frames**

The bank will electronically file a DOEP form within 30 days after the first transaction in currency that the bank wishes to exempt.

**Monitoring**

The bank will perform an annual review to determine if a customer remains eligible for the Phase II exempt status.  Information supporting eligibility will be maintained.  It will also review and verify that Phase II accounts are monitored for suspicious activity. The Bank is not required to record or report a change of control in a designated or payroll customer.

**Revocation of Exemption**

The bank may but is not required to file a DOEP form when an exemption is revoked.  The filing of CTRs provides adequate notice to the government that the bank no longer considers this customer to be an exempt person.

**Limitation of Liability of Failure to File CTRs**

No bank is liable for failure to file a report with respect to a currency transaction by an exempt person unless the bank:

* Knowingly provides false or incomplete information with respect to the transaction or the customer engaging in the transaction or;  
    
* Has reason to believe that the customer does not qualify as an exempt customer.

**Effect on Other Regulatory Requirements**

Exemption procedures do not change the requirements that the bank file SARs or its obligation to retain records of funds transfers by a person or in the connection of the sale of monetary instruments to a person.

**Monetary Instruments**

The bank is required to verify the identity of persons purchasing monetary instruments between $3,000 and $10,000 inclusive and to maintain records of all such purchases.

Monetary instruments include:

* Bank checks or drafts (including foreign drafts)  
    
* Cashier's checks

The bank only sells cashier’s checks to established customers.   

The bank is currently using the **“Official Document Report”** to maintain the required records. 

**Verification of Purchaser**

Verification may be performed by: 

* Checking a signature card on file or other record at the bank, (CIP information) or any other record indicating that the employee verified the account holder's identity at the time the customer opened the account or any other subsequent time. Or  
    
* By viewing a form of acceptable identification containing the purchaser’s name and address (unexpired driver’s license or state identification card).  
    
* If the purchaser is an elderly or disabled person who does not possess a normally acceptable form of identification (driver’s license or state identification card), the bank will require two forms of identification as specified in the CIP.

If the purchaser cannot provide the required information at the time of the purchase or cannot be identified through bank records, the transaction is to be refused.

**Purchaser with a Deposit Account**

The following information is required for record retention:

* Purchaser's name.  
    
* Date of purchase.  
    
* Type(s), Serial number(s), and the dollar amount of each of the instrument(s) purchased in currency.  
    
* Account holder’s identity verification; account number or document issuer and number information (state of issuance and driver’s license or id number).

**Simultaneous Purchases** 

Simultaneous purchases of the same or different types of instruments totaling $3,000 or more must be treated as one purchase.  Multiple purchases during one business day totaling $3000 or more must be aggregated and treated as one purchase if the bank has knowledge that the purchases have occurred. If there are aggregate sales but the bank does not know about them until after the sale, the bank must identify the sale as an aggregate sale.

If a person purchases a negotiable instrument for just under $3,000, but a service fee is added that brings the total to $3,000 or more, and the total amount is paid in currency, then the identification and recordkeeping requirements must be met.   

**Indirect Currency Purchases of Monetary Instruments**

If a customer purchases a monetary instrument between $3,000 and $10,000 with currency and deposits the currency into their account first, the transaction is still subject to recordkeeping requirements.[^12]

**Wire Transfers**

The bank is required to collect and record certain information in connection with funds transfers of $3000 or more and transmittal orders[^13] for funds transfers of $3000 or more.

Incoming and outgoing wires are only completed for established customers.

Refer to the bank’s **Wire Transfer Policy or Wire Transfer Procedures** for more detail

**Originator's Bank**

The following records for established customers[^14] must be obtained and recorded: 

* Name and address of the originator  
    
* Amount of the funds transfer  
    
* Date of the funds transfer  
    
* Any payment instructions received from the originator  
    
* The identity of the beneficiary's bank  
    
* As many of the following items the customer gives the bank with the payment order:  
    
  * Name and address of the beneficiary  
      
  * Account number of the beneficiary


  * Any other specific identifier of the beneficiary

**Funds Transfer**

If the funds transfer order is made in person, the employee taking the order must verify the identity of the person placing the order before it can be accepted.

The following records must be obtained and recorded:

* All required established customer information  
    
* Name and address of the person placing the funds transfer  
    
* Type of identification reviewed  
    
* Number of the identification document (e.g., driver's license)   
    
* The person's taxpayer identification number or, if none, the person's alien identification number or passport number and country of issuance, or a notation of the lack of such number  
    
* The originator's (if different from the person placing the order and the bank has knowledge that the person placing the payment order is not the originator) taxpayer identification number or, if none, the person's alien identification number or passport number and country of issuance, or a notation of the lack of such number

If the funds transfer order is not made in person, the following records must be obtained and recorded:

* All required established customer information  
    
* Name and address of the person placing the funds transfer  
    
* The person’s taxpayer identification number or, if none, the person's alien identification number or passport number and country of issuance, or a notation of the lack of such number  
    
* The originator's (if different from the person placing the order and the bank has knowledge that the person placing the payment order is not the originator) taxpayer identification number or, if none, the person's alien identification number or passport number and country of issuance, or a notation of the lack of such number  
    
* Copy or record of the payment method for the funds transfer

**Beneficiary's Bank**

If the bank receives a wire transfer payment order in an amount of $3,000 or more, it must retain the original or reproduction of the original payment order. 

**Transmittal Orders- “Travel Rule”**

Fund transmittals in an amount of $3,000 or more are to include the following information in the transmittal order:

* The name and, if the payment is made from an account, account number of the transmitter  
    
* Address of the transmitter  
    
* Amount of transmittal order  
    
* Date of transmittal order  
    
* Identity of the recipient's financial institution.  
    
* As many of the following items the bank receives with the transmittal order:  
  * Name and address of the recipient  
      
  * Account number of the recipient


  * Any other specific identifier of the recipient.


  * The name, address or numeric identifier of the transmitter’s financial institution.

If the bank acts as a receiving intermediary financial institution, it is to include all of the above-received information at the time the bank sends the transmittal order to the next receiving institution.  It has no duty to obtain information not provided by the transmitters’ financial institution or the preceding financial institution.

**Record Retrieval**

The Bank must keep records so that they can be retrieved by reference to:

* Originating – originator’s name or account number

* Beneficiary – beneficiary’s name or account number  
    
* There are no recordkeeping requirements in the “Travel Rule”

**Record keeping Exemptions**

Exemptions from the recordkeeping requirements include funds transfers where (a) both the originator and beneficiary are the same person and the originator's bank and the beneficiary's bank is the same domestic bank and (b) both the   
Originator and the beneficiary are any of the following:

* A domestic bank  
    
* A wholly owned domestic subsidiary of a domestic bank  
    
* A wholly owned domestic subsidiary of a domestic broker or dealer in securities  
    
* The United States  
    
* A state or local government  
    
* A federal, state or local government agency or instrumentality

**Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing**

**Money Laundering**

It is the criminal practice of processing ill-gotten gains, or “dirty’ money, through a series of transactions.  This is done so that the funds are “cleaned”; appear to be gotten from legal activities.  Currency doesn’t have to be involved in every stage of the money laundering process.  It is a diverse and often complex process.  There are three basic steps than can occur at the same time.

**Three Basic Steps**

* **Placement** – getting the money into a more convenient form.  This is the most vulnerable stage.  
* Structuring currency deposits in amounts to evade reporting requirements – dividing large amounts of currency into less-conspicuous smaller sums that are deposited directly into a bank account  
    
* Commingling currency deposits of legal and illegal enterprises

  EXAMPLE: Bubba S. is a small-time drug dealer.  His trade area is most of the rural county including seven school districts.  His clients are from 11 to 18 years old and pay him in singles, fives, tens and twenties.  His supplier insists on being paid weekly in large bills.

  The bills that Bubba receives are bulky and require more effort to conceal.  They also cannot be used to satisfy his most significant need for operating capital, the purchase of inventory.  Personally, he is at risk because everyone he deals with knows he carries large amounts of cash at all times.  He needs the cooperation of a financial institution to either perform currency exchanges or accept cash for deposit and allow withdrawals of large bills.

* **Layering** – conducting a series of transactions designed solely to make it difficult to trace the original source of funds.  It’s a more complex stage.

  EXAMPLE: Susan is the administrator of a money laundering business that handles approximately $300,000 weekly, mostly in cash, for a variety of illegal businesses.  For one client, she deposits the funds into 9 accounts for shell or fictitious companies, each at a different financial institution.  The money is wired to a bank in Atlanta for the credit of another fictitious company.  It is then wired to a non-cooperative country or territory (NCCT) and then to Spain or Columbia.  The Spanish depository wires the funds back to the US for deposit into the account of an apparently unrelated, shell company.  The Columbian depository 

* **Integration** – funds are used to acquire legitimate assets or fund further activities.  This is the ultimate stage.  It further shields the criminal from a recorded connection to the funds by providing a credible explanation of where the funds came from.

  EXAMPLE: “Lenny’s” is a pizza parlor in a major city.  Over a 12 month period its daily cash deposits are approximately $16,000.  Its depository financial institution issues an exemption from CTR filing based on this activity.  In fact, most of the money passing through the business is derived from prostitution

**Terrorist Financing**

The motivation behind terrorist financing is ideological.  Terrorism is intended to intimidate a population or compel a government or an international organization to do or abstain from doing a specific act through the threat of violence.  Money laundering is a vital part of terrorist financing.  Funding for terrorist attacks does not always require large amounts of money.  This could result in very simple transactions associated with the money.

Activities are generally financed through both unlawful and legitimate sources.  Legitimate sources are the key difference between terrorist financiers and traditional criminal organizations.

* Illegal activities include:  
* Extortion, kidnapping, and drug trafficking are major sources  
    
* Smuggling, fraud, theft, identity theft, or improper use of charitable or relief funds  
    
* Legitimate sources include:  
    
* Charitable donations, government sponsors, business ownership, and personal employment


The methods used to can be the same as or similar to those methods used by other criminals that launder funds.

* Examples: currency smuggling; structured deposits or withdrawals from bank accounts; purchases of varied types of monetary instruments; credit, debit, or stored value cards; and funds transfers.

For suspicious transactions related to terrorist activity, call the FinCEN hotline at – (866)556-3974. Don’t delay calling.  
**Suspicious Activity Reporting**

The board of directors acknowledges the bank’s responsibility to file Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) as required by federal regulations.  The bank will also file voluntary reports of criminal activity with federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies where appropriate.

The BSA compliance officer is responsible for developing written procedures and assuring that suspicious activity is identified and brought to management’s attention. 

Internal controls will be developed to monitor and bring suspicious employee or customer activity to the attention of the appropriate personnel.  All SAR filings are reported to the Board of Directors at the next scheduled meeting by the BSA compliance officer.

All employees will receive training detailing that they are responsible for identifying and reporting suspicious employee or customer activity.  Examples of suspicious activity and instructions will be given to employees to allow them to identify and report suspicious activity to the appropriate officer.  Emphasis will be given to the fact that reporting any suspicious activity is a requirement of bank policy and failure to comply can lead to termination.

**Refer to Appendix I for examples of suspicious activity**

**Federal regulation requirements to file a SAR:**

* Criminal violations involving insider abuse in any amount  
    
* Criminal violations aggregating $5,000 or more when a suspect can be identified  
    
* Criminal violations aggregating $25,000 or more regardless of a potential suspect  
    
* Transactions[^15] conducted or attempted of $5,000 or more if the bank knows, suspects, or has reason to suspect that the transaction involves the following:


  * May involve potential money laundering or other illegal activity  
      
  * Is designed to evade the requirements of BSA


  * Has no business or apparent lawful purpose or is not the type of transaction that the particular customer would normally be expected to engage in, and the bank knows of no reasonable explanation for the transaction after examining the available facts, including the background and possible purpose of the transaction.


**Filing Time Frames and Record Retention**

The SAR must be filed no later than 30 calendar days from the date of the initial detection of facts that may form a basis for filing a SAR.  If no suspect can be identified, the time period is extended to 60 days. The time period for filing a SAR starts when the organization, during its review or because of other factors, knows or has reason to suspect that the activity or transactions under review meet one or more of the definitions of suspicious activity.

Reports will be filed electronically. 

In situations involving violations requiring immediate attention, such as when a reportable violation is ongoing, the bank will immediately notify, by telephone, an appropriate law enforcement authority (IRS Criminal Investigation Division or FBI) and the FDIC in addition to filing a timely SAR.

The bank will retain a copy of the SAR and all supporting documentation for five years from the filing date.

**Continuing Activity Filing**

If the initial activity continues over a time period, the bank should continue to file a SAR at least every 90 days. The review is for 90 days from the prior filing and the SAR is to be filed within 30 days from the review. Law enforcement may have an interest in making sure that certain accounts remain open.  If requested to do so, the bank will require a written request.  The bank has the right to make the ultimate decision to maintain or close the account.

**Safe Harbor** 

Federal law provides protection for the bank from civil liability in connection with all SAR filings regardless of whether the SAR is voluntary or mandatory. The protection encompasses the bank and its directors, officers, employees, and agents.

**SAR Sharing within the Corporate Structure**

The bank may disclose a SAR to its controlling company or companies no matter where the entity is located.  At this time, this item does not pertain to the bank.  When and if it does, procedures will be put in place including a written confidentiality agreement to protect the shared information.  

**Law Enforcement Inquiries and Requests**

Inquiries and requests can include grand jury subpoenas, National Security Letters (NSLs), and section 314(a) requests.  All requests should be directed to senior management and the BSA compliance officer.  The decision to file a SAR will be made after further investigation of the account(s).  The receipt of these types of inquiries or requests should not be included in the SAR narration.

All requests are to be documented on the “Law Enforcement Customer Documentation Requests” form located on the BSA Compliance Officers Drive on the bank’s network.  

**SAR Confidentiality**

No bank director, officer, employee, or agent of a bank that reports a suspicious transaction may notify any person involved in the transaction that a SAR has been filed.  Any person subpoenaed or otherwise requested to disclose a SAR or the information contained in a SAR, except when such disclosure is requested by FinCEN or appropriate law enforcement or federal banking agency, will decline to produce the SAR or provide any information that would disclose that a SAR has been prepared or filed.  

The President/CEO will disclose specific information contained in any reported SAR as requested by the board of directors.

**Key Suspicious Activity Monitoring Components**

* Identification of Unusual Activity  
  * Employee Identification  
      
  * Law Enforcement Inquiries and Requests


  * National Security Letters (NSL)  
      
  * Transaction and Surveillance Monitoring Systems

    

* Alert Management	


* SAR Decision Making  
  * SAR Completion and Filing

**Suspicious Activity Reporting Procedures**

* Any employee having knowledge of any suspicious activity or unusual transactions conducted or attempted by a customer, a fellow employee, officer or director of the bank will notify the BSA or assistant BSA compliance officer by completing a “Suspicious Activity Reporting Form”.  The form is available on the “P” drive under teller documents.  
    
* Computer or Network related intrusions should first be reported to the BSA officer or senior management. They will report to the systems administrator.   
    
* The BSA compliance officer will complete the “Suspicious Activity Investigation Report” and conduct a preliminary investigation to determine if there is any evidence that may indicate illegal activity.  If no evidence is found, no further action is needed.    
    
* If the BSA compliance officer determines that there may be evidence of illegal activity, he/she will gather as much information as possible from bank records and make a summary of the information. Questioning of employees will be thorough and employees will be informed that no one will get back with them on any follow up actions.  A number will be used to replace the customer’s name in the document for reporting and filing purposes.  
    
* A committee composed of the BSA compliance officer, comptroller and the President CEO (along with legal counsel if needed) will review the information and determine if a SAR filing is required.   
    
  * If no filing is required, a notation will be made on the summary and retained until the next on-site FDIC examination and then destroyed.  
      
  * If filing is required, the SAR form is to be filed promptly.  The information in the form will only include information directly relevant to the suspicious activity.  


  * All supporting documentation is filed by number and retained for five years.


  


* The filing is reported by the BSA compliance officer to the board of directors at the next regularly scheduled meeting.

Terminating Relationships with SAR Subjects

* The SAR committee will review the account(s) prior to filing to determine whether the account should be closed at filing or remain open.  
    
* The SAR committee will consider the following factors when deciding if an account relationship should be terminated or restricted:  
    
* The length of the customer relationship – Because most check related fraud occurs after the first thirty days the account is opened, a customer relationship that is open for less than six months will be considered a new account relationship in terms of suspicious activity.  An established customer will be any customer relationship which has been open and in good standing for longer than one year.  
    
* The dollar amount of risk posed to the Bank – If the Bank is at risk for monetary loss, the BSA officer may restrict the account after discussion with Senior Management before presenting the activity to the SAR committee.  
    
* Whether the Bank would be subject to legal actions, enforcement actions, or reputation risk.  
    
* If the customer is a High-Risk Customer and the account would require additional monitoring.  
    
* If the customer has deliberately mislead the bank for any reason.  
    
    
* If the SAR committee determines that the risk to the bank is significant, the committee has the right to decide if a relationship will be restricted or terminated.  Upon deciding to restrict or terminate, the BSA officer will notify the officer listed on the account, or if no officer is listed, the BSA officer will notify management.  An official notification will be sent to the account holder the day the relationship is terminated.  If an account is restricted or terminated because of a risk based factor, a note will be attached to the account holder’s customer information file that states “Do Not Re-Open Accounts” to avoid any future BSA/AML related risk.

Elder Financial Exploitation

All employees need to report elder exploitation to the BSA Officer.

Older Americans hold a high concentration of wealth as compared to the general population. In the instances where elderly individuals experience declining cognitive or physical abilities, they may find themselves more reliant on specific individuals for their physical well-being, financial management, and social interaction. While anyone can be a victim of a financial crime such as identity theft, embezzlement, and fraudulent schemes, certain elderly individuals may be particularly vulnerable. 

The following red flags could indicate the existence of elder financial exploitation. This list of red flags identifies only *possible* signs of illicit activity. 

• Erratic or unusual banking transactions or changes in banking patterns: 

* Frequent large withdrawals, including daily maximum currency withdrawals from an ATM; 

* Sudden Non-Sufficient Fund activity; 

* Uncharacteristic nonpayment for services, which may indicate a loss of funds or access to funds; 

* Debit transactions that are inconsistent for the elder; 

* Uncharacteristic attempts to wire large sums of money; 

* Closing of CDs or accounts without regard to penalties. 

• Interactions with customers or caregivers:

* A caregiver or other individual shows excessive interest in the elder’s finances or assets, does not allow the elder to speak for himself, or is reluctant to leave the elder’s side during conversations; 

* The elder shows an unusual degree of fear or submissiveness toward a caregiver, or expresses a fear of eviction or nursing home placement if money is not given to a caretaker; 

* The financial institution is unable to speak directly with the elder, despite repeated attempts to contact him or her; 

* A new caretaker, relative, or friend suddenly begins conducting financial transactions on behalf of the elder without proper documentation; 

* The customer moves away from existing relationships and toward new associations with other “friends” or strangers; 

* The elderly individual’s financial management changes suddenly, such as through a change of power of attorney to a different family member or a new individual; 

* The elderly customer lacks knowledge about his or her financial status or shows a sudden reluctance to discuss financial matters.  
    
* If suspicious activity concerning elder abuse will file a SAR.

Human Trafficking

Human Trafficking is a modern-day form of slavery involving the illegal trade of people for exploitation or commercial gain.  
As customers and financial transactions pass through the institution, all personnel will be trained to identify signs of human trafficking. There are a number of red flags that can specifically indicate human trafficking.

Red Flags for Tellers and Personnel

* Round dollar payroll checks being cashed by ladies scantily dressed and accompanied by a man 


* The same ladies presenting foreign ID cards including international drivers’ licenses and valid passports with no visa or an expired visa, or even a student ID  
    
* Evidence of being controlled  
    
* Bruises or other signs of physical abuse


* Fear or depression

* Not speaking on his/her own behalf and/or non-English speaking  
    
* Having another “handler” answering questions, holding ID, and governing the person cashing a check

  **Note**: The “handler” may not be at the teller window but may be in the lobby observing (as a driver, friend, etc.) or in the parking lot.

Reg Flags for Account Opening Personnel

* Be aware of business types that are prone to human trafficking such as hotels, restaurants, truck stops, cleaning/janitorial services, massage parlors, nail salons, etc.


  


* Articles of Incorporation from other states, such as Florida, California, New York, Nevada or others known as major trafficking states. 


* Addresses for businesses being a P.O. Box or the physical address of a postal store often found in a strip mall  
    
* Inconsistent dates between ID and business documentation (their ID indicates they lived in our state for several years, but the business physical address was another state for the same period of time)  
    
* Address of the business is in an apartment complex  
    
* Multiple unrelated people living at the same address

Red Flags for BSA/AML Personnel

* Rent payments for multiple locations within apartment complexes


* Rent payments made by unrelated business types (e.g., a cleaning business paying rent for several apartments)  
    
* New businesses making tons of money overnight  
    
* Round dollar payroll checks being cashed  
    
* ACH credits received from an international trading site for imports/exports

Check Card Debits

* From an airline and/or moving company


* For rent payments for related things involving trafficking such as gas, beer/liquor, restaurants, casinos, a gentlemen’s club, etc.  
    
* Occurring in various cities over a short period of time such as a month

SARS

Whenever red flags of any type are spotted, it is the responsibility of the Bank Secrecy Act/anti-money laundering (BSA/AML) officer to investigate and determine if the activity is suspicious. If it is, the activity is to be reported via a suspicious activity report (SAR). The will enable law enforcement to pursue a case against the traffickers and put a stop to their crimes. This partnership with law enforcement does not need to end with filing a SAR. Communicate with law enforcement personally.

**Comparison with Government Lists**

Government list searches may be accomplished by interdiction software with a third-party database, the use of the bank’s Customer Information File (CIF) application or other internal reports.

**List of Known Terrorists or Terrorist Organizations**

The bank will take the necessary steps to compare customer’s information with any government lists of known terrorists or terrorist organizations as described in Section 326 of the USA Patriot Act.  There is no such list at this time.

**OFAC List**

Comparison to the OFAC list is accomplished by, but not limited to the following:

* www.instantofac.com  
* Outgoing and incoming wire transfer originators, beneficiaries, banks and countries are compared prior to final processing.  
* New customer loan accounts are verified via credit report  
* The BSA compliance officer reviews the monthly system generated OFAC report for possible matches.

**Possible Match Procedures**

* Notify the BSA or assistant BSA compliance officer.   
    
* The BSA compliance officer will compare data received from the customer to determine a possible match.    
    
* If there are sufficient discrepancies to disprove the match, the notification will be disregarded.  Notations will be made on the daily OFAC report to such effect.  
    
* If there are a number of similarities or an exact match, call the OFAC Hotline at 800-540-6322. Record that the call was made. The BSA or assistant BSA compliance officer and a senior member of management will determine if the account or transaction must be blocked or rejected based on instructions from OFAC. 

**Blocking or Rejecting Procedures**

Blocked funds: 

* Funds will be placed in an interest-bearing deposit account from which only OFAC authorized debits may be made. The account will be flagged to prevent debits. The interest rate will be at a rate currently offered to other depositors on deposits of comparable size and maturity.  
    
* Normal service charges may be debited from the account.  
    
    
* No set-offs are permitted  
    
* Escheat to the state is permitted if the state obtains a license  
    
* The blocking or rejecting will be reported to OFAC within ten (10) business days.  There is no model form but regulation requires reports contain the following information:  
    
    
  * Blocked transaction- identity of the owner or account party, property, location of the property, account number or other identifier for the property, actual or estimated value, date of the blocking, a photocopy of the transfer or payment instructions and the name and address of the blocking institution  
      
  * Rejected item – the name and address of the transferee financial institution, the date and amount of the transfer, a copy of the payment or transfer instructions received and the basis for the rejection.


    

* The BSA Officer will report blocked or rejected transactions to:


  * Senior Management – after the initial report is filed with OFAC  
      
  * Board of Directors – at the Board meeting following the filing

 

**Reporting Requirements**

* All blocked and rejected transactions must be reported to OFAC within ten (10) business days of the occurrence.  
    
* All blocked transactions must be reported annually by September 30 for all assets blocked as of June 30\.  Form TD F 90-22.50 is to be used.   

**Record Retention Requirements**

* Rejected transactions – the complete and accurate record for at least five (5) years after the date of the transaction  
    
* Blocked property or transactions – for the period the property is blocked and for five (5) years after the date the property is unblocked.

**Information Sharing**

A federal, state, local, or foreign law enforcement agency investigating terrorist or money laundering may request FinCEN to solicit, on its behalf, certain information from a financial institution or a group of financial institutions. FinCEN may require the bank to search its records to determine whether certain individuals, entities or organizations have or had accounts with the bank, or engaged in transactions with it.  The bank must conduct a one-time search of its records to identify any named suspects. FinCEN sends these requests every two weeks.

**Request Procedures**

* The BSA and assistant BSA compliance officers and other employees that are approved by the Board of Directors are designated to receive 314(a) requests via the Secure Information Sharing System (SISS).  
    
* The bank is required to search its customer data base in: deposit records, funds transfer records subject to mandatory record retention (originators and recipients only), monetary instrument sales subject to mandatory record retention (remitters only), loan records, and safe deposit records.  
    
* Transactions linked to an account should be searched for the preceding 12 months.  Transactions not linked to an account should be researched for the preceding 6 months.  
    
* The Bank maintains a written policy that incoming and outgoing wires and the sale of monetary instruments are limited only to established customers.  The search performed on the bank’s master customer data base meets the screening requirements for these areas.   
    
* The BSA compliance officer will notify matches to FinCEN via the Secure Information Sharing System within 14 days unless otherwise specified in the information request. A copy of the email sent to FinCEN and the supporting documents will be retained in the 314(a)-request file.  No other details are provided other than there is a match.  A negative response is not required.  
    
* The BSA compliance officer will document completed searches and retain records until after the most current FDIC BSA exam.

**Information Safeguards**

Confidentiality and security are very important and access to requests and records should be limited.
