* All information will be saved on the BSA Compliance Officers file under their network drive. Only those approved to access the FinCEN 314(a) web address will be allowed to access these folders.  
    
* Information concerning any requests will not be conveyed to anyone other than those allowed to access the web address.  
    
* Any copies of documents will be destroyed via shredding.

**SAR Filing**

If a match is determined, the BSA Compliance Officer will review the customer’s file to determine if any illegal activity or risk is present.  The SAR committee will review the file and determine whether a SAR should be filed.

This regulation allows the bank to register with FinCEN to share information; we believe may relate to money laundering or terrorist financing, with other registered financial institutions.  

The bank is not currently registered but the BSA compliance officer has the authority to register under appropriate circumstances.  Prior to registering the BSA Officer shall implement procedures for sending and receiving information requests.  The BSA Officer must establish that the recipient bank is registered with FinCEN and implement security measures which are adequate to use and protect information obtained from other financial institutions.

   

**Foreign Activities**

**Foreign Correspondent Accounts**

The bank does not deal in foreign correspondent accounts at this time.  If and when it decides to deal with these types of accounts, policies and procedures will be developed to make sure that all statutory and regulatory guidelines are followed.

**Private Banking Accounts – Non-U.S. Persons**

The bank does not deal in private banking accounts for non-U.S. persons at this time.  If and when it decides to deal with these types of accounts, policies and procedures will be developed to make sure that all statutory and regulatory guidelines are followed.

**Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts** 

The bank will file a Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts (FBAR) with FinCEN Form 114 annually on or before June 30 for foreign financial accounts exceeding $10,000.

The bank does not deal in these types of accounts at this time.

**International Transportation of Currency of Monetary Instruments**

The bank will file FinCEN form 105 for currency or monetary instruments for amounts exceeding $10,000 shipped in and out of the U.S. by bank personnel.

The bank has not completed this type of transaction to date.

**Customer Identification Requirements and Verification**

On October 26, 2001, the USA Patriot Act was signed into law and added several new provisions to the Bank Secrecy Act requirements in reference to identifying our customers.   These guidelines and procedures are intended to ensure that the bank reasonably knows the true identity of each customer[^16] and has processes that identify and assess the risk of new customers and accounts through appropriate customer due diligence.  Procedures are based on risk assessment of the types of accounts offered, the methods used to open new accounts, the various types of identifying information available, and the bank’s size, location, loss history, and customer base.

The CIP applies to all areas of the bank.  The purpose of this program is to implement reasonable procedures for (2) verifying the identity of any person seeking to open an account, to the extent reasonable and practicable; (2) maintain records of the information used to verify the persons identify, including name, address, and other identifying information, and (3) determining whether the person appears on any lists of known or suspected terrorists or terrorist organizations provided by the any government agency.   

**Customer Information Standards**

Prior to account opening, each account owner named on the account must provide the required basic information.  These requirements may not be waived under any circumstance. For NEW customers, if the required information is not provided, the account will not be opened.  For EXISTING customers, it is recommended that current customer information be verified to the CIF.

**Exceptions**

As allowed by law, CIP will not be applied to customers who are 

* federal, state, or local government entities, nor to companies whose common stock is listed on the NY or American Stock Exchange or on the NASDAQ National Market System (other than the small-capitalization segment of the NASDAQ NMS)  
    
* a financial institution that is subject to regulation by a Federal Regulator, or a bank regulated by a State Bank regulator 

**Required Basic Information**   
**Individual Persons** – The following information must be obtained from: an individual who opens an account; an individual who opens an account by or on behalf of another person or entity[^17]; or from account signatories or business owners:

* Legal name (first, middle, last and maiden name where appropriate)  
    
* Address (physical and/or mailing address where different)[^18]  
    
* Date of Birth  
    
* U.S. person tax payer identification number (SSN)

In no instance, will an account be opened without a taxpayer identification number. 

**Businesses/Entities** – The following information must be obtained from a business or commercial entity of any nature that opens an account:

* Legal name and any additional (DBA) names  
    
* Address[^19]   
    
* U.S. tax payer identification number[^20] (EIN or SSN for sole proprietorships)

In no instance, will an account be opened without a taxpayer identification number. 

**Non-U.S. Persons** –Individuals who do not possess a U.S.TIN  
Bank officer approval must be obtained first and then you must obtain one or more of the following:

* Taxpayer identification number  
    
* Passport number and country of issuance  
    
* Alien identification card number  
* Number and country of issuance of any other government-issued document evidencing nationality or residence and bearing a photograph or similar safeguard

**Applied for TIN**

The bank may make exceptions for persons applying for a taxpayer identification number if the bank can reasonably verify that application has been made.  

* In the case involving an individual, the account may be opened but must have the taxpayer identification number of a secondary owner, i.e., parent or guardian.     
* If the customer is a business who has applied for a TIN, the account may be opened as soon as the TIN is received. 

 

**45 Day Waiting Period**

Missing TINs for an authorized signer will be placed on a pending list and tracked for forty-five days.  If the TIN is not received within the forty-five-day period, the person will be notified of removal from the account.  Exceptions may apply in the case of newborns or minors when the Bank has proof that the TIN has been requested.

**Verification of Customer Identification** 

The bank will take risk based steps to verify the identity of all persons that open an account[^21] with the bank**.**  These steps will vary depending on the type of account being opened and the type of identity record being verified. Verification methods may be documentary or non-documentary.

* Verification should be performed at account opening or within a reasonable time frame after the account is opened, generally within a week.   
* Verification for loan customers is required when an application is approved.  
* If initial verification does not confirm identity, and additional customer due diligence does not confirm the identity of the “person”, the BSA Officer may close the account after review. 

Verification procedures will not be required for existing customers if the bank has reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of the customer.

**Documentary Verification**

Documents that may be used for verification may include but are not limited to the lists shown below.  Customers having types of identifying documents that are unknown to the bank may be subject to further verification steps, or the account may not be considered for opening.  In these cases, a bank officer’s approval will be required before opening.

**Individuals**

Individual customers include all named persons with a present ownership interest in an account.  For individual accounts held for the benefit of minor, informal groups, etc., the account signatory should be treated as the individual customer.

**Minimum documentation (primary and secondary)**

The customer should provide at least one (1) of the following primary identification documents:

* Unexpired government-issued driver’s license showing an address and a photo  
    
* Unexpired government-issued identification card showing an address and a photo (state ID card, military ID card, U.S. Alien registration card)  
    
* Unexpired government-issued passport showing nationality and residence and a photo  
    
* If a customer is unable to produce a primary ID, the account will not be opened. At least one (1) of the following secondary identification documents must be obtained:    
    
* Social Security Card  
    
* Medicare/Medicaid Card  
    
* State issued and certified birth certificate  
    
* Student ID (should include a picture)  
    
* Insurance card  
    
* Utility Bill (must include customer’s name and current address)  
    
* Concealed weapon licenses  
    
* Other documentation approved by an officer

**Businesses/Entities**

**Minimum documentation**

* Corporation, LLC, LLP – evidence of good standing with the state\*[^22]  
    
* General Partnership – partnership agreement or business license or assumed name certificate or current tax return  
    
* Sole Proprietorship – business license or assumed name certificate and standard ID for the owner  
    
* Clubs or Civic Organizations – meeting minutes identifying authorized signers or officer roster with current phone number listing and standard ID for the person opening the account  
    
* Estate or Guardianship \- certified copy of the court order naming the personal representative   
    
* Formal Trust – written certification form executed by the attorney who prepared the trust or first and last page containing signatures of the trust agreement

**Non-documentary Verification**

Non-documentary methods of verifying identification may include but are not limited to the following:

**Individuals**

* Public data bases  
    
* Credit bureaus  
    
* Sending thank you notes  
    
* Calling the customer at provided phone numbers

B**usinesses/Entities**

* Financial reports  
    
* Prior bank references or current bank statements  
    
* Public data bases  
    
* Visiting the place of business

**Customer Notice**

The bank will provide notice to all customers before an account is opened that adequately describes the reasons for obtaining and verifying the customer’s identity.  This notice can be provided to the customer by posting it in the lobby.

**Suspicious Activity Reporting**

If a potential customer uses unusual or suspicious identification documents that can’t be readily verified, gives false information, uses a false tax id number or social security number, the employee suspecting or collecting the information will notify the BSA Compliance Officer via the “Suspected Suspicious Activity’’ form.  

**Refer to the Suspicious Activity Reporting Section**

**Customer Due Diligence**

Due diligence is the process of obtaining information at account opening and during the life of the account that will help the bank in identifying potentially high-risk customers.  When a high-risk customer is identified, it is subject to enhanced due diligence.

The bank uses various means to determine the risk associated with a customer and account.  The following list is used but not limited to:

* Deposit accounts –paper risk assessment worksheet, paper business profile, and “ChexSystems/Qualifile” application.    
    
* Loan accounts – credit reports and paper CIP Worksheets, 

**Considering the Account**

The bank is under no obligation to open an account even if identification is verified. If the banker opening the account suspects the account is being opened under fraudulent reasons or suspicious activity, the account should not be opened. Notify the BSA compliance officer for further instructions.

The banker opening the account should consider the following factors:

* Purpose of opening the account.  
    
* The customer's residence location. If outside trade area, why did customer select Louisa Community Bank? Outside trade area residency may be acceptable, but should be scrutinized.  
  * The source of funds. Initial Deposits of significant amounts of cash should be questioned as to how the cash was obtained. The answer must be reasonable. 


  * Existing and prior bank references. 


  * Any requests by the customer for inclusion on the exemption list. Such requests should be scrutinized closely and elevated to the senior management level.

**Assigning Risk**

Identification of individuals and new entities (businesses) with possible increased risk will occur at account opening.   

Risk ratings will be assigned as:

* HIGH    
    
* MEDIUM   
    
* LOW 

An **Individual Risk Assessment & Due Diligence** form will be used to determine the rating for individual accounts opened in branches.  

The following account types will initially be assigned a High-risk code:

* Business customer **that has been rated** in a high-risk category**[^23].**  
    
* Money market and savings accounts that are located in high intensity drug trafficking or high financial crimes area, or high geographic area, or have hits on OFAC.

Customer Due Diligence and CIP procedures will be applied to all Business accounts.  The CIP procedures are specific to the type of entity.  CIP is performed on all signatories. \*  Customer Due Diligence includes verification of business entity via Secretary of State website or legal documents and completion of CIP Business Profile and Business Risk Assessment.  In the event that an account is opened for an MSB or NBFI, more forms and documentation are required.  
\* Exceptions – Federal, State, or local government entities, or companies on the NY or American Stock Exchange or NASDAQ, or a financial institution that is regulated by a Federal or State regulator.

Business saving and certificate of deposit accounts will be risk rated low.  
Loan accounts will be assigned a LOW BSA risk code.  If the bank wide assessment or the customer’s individual assessment changes the risk code may change.

Loan officers are instructed to contact the BSA compliance officer via the “Suspicious Activity Incident Report” form of any suspicious activity.    
Risk codes may be re-evaluated and changed or assigned as deemed necessary by the BSA compliance officer.   
**Beneficial Ownership**

The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) issued the Customer Due Diligence Requirements for Financial Institutions Rule (CDD Rule) in May 2016\. 

The CDD Rule requires us to establish procedures that enable us to identify the beneficial owners of each customer as the time a new account is opened, unless the customer is otherwise excluded or the transaction is exempted.  The procedures must establish risk-based practices verifying the identity of each beneficial owner identified, to the extent reasonable and practicable.  The procedures must contain the elements required for verifying the identities of customers that are individuals under applicable Customer Identification Program (CIP) requirements (i.e. We are required to obtain, verify and record the identity of the beneficial owners of legal entity customers.

The CDD Rule is applicable to “new” accounts.  The Rule defines a new account as each account opened as covered financial institution by a legal entity customer on or after May 11, 2018\.

The Rule does not supersede existing obligations and practices regarding trust.  While we are not required to look through a trust to its beneficiaries, we may need to take additional steps to verify the identity of a customer that is not an individual, such as obtaining information about the persons with control over the account.  When a trust is our direct customer, we will identify and verify the identity of trustees because trustees will be signatories on trust accounts.  Additionally, in the case of revocable trusts, we may need to gather information about the settlor, grantor, trustee or other persons with the authority to direct the trustee and who thus has the authority or control over the account, in order to establish the trust identity of the customer.

**Beneficial Owner-Defined**

The CDD defines a beneficial owner as each of the following

* Each individual, if any, who directly or indirectly, owns 25% or more of the equity interests of a legal entity customer (OWNERSHIP PRONG).  
* A single individual with significant responsibility to control, manage or direct a legal entity customer, including an executive officer or senior manager (e.g. CEO, CFO, COO, Managing Member, General Partner, President, Vice President or Treasurer); or any other individual who regularly performs similar functions (CONTROL PRONGS).

**Collection of Beneficial Ownership**

The CDD Rule requires us to obtain information about the beneficial owners of a legal entity from the individual seeking to open a new account on behalf of the legal entity customer.  This individual could, but would not necessarily, be a beneficial owner. Please refer to the **Beneficial Ownership Account Opening Procedures** to guide you through the account opening process and the **Customer Identification Program Procedures** for information required to be obtained from each beneficial owner (i.e. CIP procedures for individuals) and acceptable documentary and non-documentary methods of verifying an individual’s identity.

**OFAC Compliance**

We must use beneficial ownership information as we use other information we gather regarding customers, including compliance with OFAC-administered sanctions.  As such, we must screen each beneficial owner against the OFAC list prior to account opening and/or disbursement of loan proceeds.

**Section 314(a) Information Sharing**

FinCEN does not expect the information obtained under the CDD Rule to add additional 314(a) requirements.  The regulation implementing Section 314(a) does not require the reporting of beneficial ownership information associated with an account or transaction matching a named subject in a 314(a) request.  We are required to search our records for accounts or transactions matching a named subject and report whether a match exists using the identifying information provided in the request.  

**Exemptions and Limitations**

We are not required to identify and verify the identity of the beneficial owners of a legal entity customer when the customer opens any of the following

* Accounts established at the point-of-sale to provide credit products, solely for the purchase of retail goods and/or services at these retailers, up to a limit of $50,000.  
* Accounts established to finance the purchase of postage and for which payments are remitted directly by the FI to the provider of the postage products.  
* Accounts established to finance insurance premiums and for which payments are remitted directly by the FI to the insurance provider or broker.  
* Accounts established to finance the purchase or lease of equipment and for which payments are remitted directly by the FI to the vendor or lessor of the equipment.

These exemptions will **NOT** apply under **EITHER** of the following circumstances:

* If the accounts are transaction accounts through which a legal entity customer can make payments to, or receive payments from, third parties.  
* If there is the possibility of a cash refund for accounts opened to finance purchase of postage, insurance premium or equipment leasing.

Additionally, the Rule excludes from the definition of legal entity customer certain entities that are subject to Federal and State regulation and for which information about their beneficial ownership and management is available from the Federal and State agencies. The following list of exclusions have been outlined in the Rule

* Financial institutions regulated by a Federal functional regulator or a bank regulated by a State bank regulator  
* Department or agency of the U.S., of any State, or of any political subdivision of a State  
* Any entity established under the laws of the U.S., or any State, or any political subdivision of any State, or under an interstate compact  
* Any entity (other than a bank) whose common stock or analogous equity interests are listed on the New York, American, or NASDAQ stock exchange  
* Any entity organized under the laws of the U.S. or of any State at least 51% of whose common stock or analogous equity interests are held by a listed entity  
* Issuers of securities registered under Section 21 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 or that is required to file reports under 15(d) of that Act  
* An SEC-registered investment adviser, as defined in Section 202(a)(11) of the investment Advisers Act of 1940  
* An exchange or clearing agency, as defined in Section 3 of the SEA, registered under Section 6 or 17A of that Act  
* Any other entity registered with the SEC under the SEA  
* A registered entity, commodity pool operator, commodity trading adviser, retail foreign exchange dealer, swap dealer on major swap participant, defined in Section 1a of the Commodity Exchange Act, registered with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission   
* A public accounting firm registered under Section 102 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act  
* A bank holding company, as defined in Section 2 of the Bank Holding Company Act of 1956 or savings and loan holding company, as defined in Section 10(n) of the Home Owners’ Loan Act  
* A pooled investment vehicle operated or advised by an Fi excluded from the definition of legal entity customer under the CDD Rule  
* An insurance company regulated by a State  
* A financial market utility designated by the Financial Stability Oversight Council under Title VIII of the DFA  
* A foreign financial institution established in a jurisdiction where the regulator of such an institution maintains beneficial ownership information regarding such institution  
* A non-U.S. governmental department, agency or political subdivision that engages only in governmental rather than commercial activities  
* Any legal entity only to the extent that is open a private banking account subject to 21 CFR 1010.620

**Beneficial Ownership Account Opening Procedures**

When an individual is opening an account on behalf of a legal entity (i.e. a business) and that business is a corporation, limited liability company, other entity created by the filing of a public document with a Secretary of State, a general partnership, a limited partnership, business trust and any similar entity formed under the laws of a foreign jurisdiction, we must obtain document information about the legal entity’s beneficial owners.

STEP 1: Determine whether the account is being opened on behalf of a legal entity.: A corporation, limited liability company, other entity created by the filing of a public document with a Secretary of State, a general partnership, a limited partnership, business trust any similar entity formed under the laws of a foreign jurisdiction.

STEP 2: Provide the Beneficial Ownership Certification From to the individual opening the account.

STEP 3: Explain the purpose of the form and provide instructions to the individual opening the account on how to complete each section.  Be sure to explain how the beneficial owners are defined under each test: by ownership and by control.  
**Refer to the Beneficial Ownership Account Opening Reference Guide for details.**

STEP 4: Ask for identification for each beneficial owner.  Photocopies and other reproductions are acceptable.   
STEP 5: Review the completed Beneficial Ownership Certification Form to ensure all fields have been completed.  Make sure **ALL** beneficial owners under the OWNERSHIP PRONG have been identified and **AT LEAST ONE** beneficial owner under the CONTROL PRONG has been identified.  Be sure the following has been collected for each beneficial owner. Name, address, date of birth and social security number or other government identification number (passport number or alien identification number).  Make sure the identities of each beneficial owner have been verified.  Make a photocopy of the identifying documents.

If no one individual owns 25% or more of the legal entity customer, there may be no beneficial owners listed under the ownership prong.

If a trust owns 25% or more of the business, use the trustee as the beneficial owner. If another business entity owns 25% or more of th4e business, inquire about the owners of the business entity that maintains ownership.  Remember, this rule is about getting to the humans behind the business.

STEP 6: Enter the information into the system.  Be sure to enter the name(s) of beneficial owner(s) into the appropriate fields within the system.

\[PROCEDURAL NOTE: The CDD Rule requires you to establish procedures that enable you to identify the beneficial owners of each customer at the time a new account is opened, unless the customer is otherwise excluded or the transaction is exempted.  The procedures must establish risk-based practices for verifying the identity of each beneficial owner identified, to the extent reasonable and practicable.  You must satisfy CIP requirements and verify the identities of the beneficial owners using documentary and/or non-documentary evidence. You will run each beneficial owner through ChexSystems to further verify their identities and performing OFAC screening.

STEP 7:  Screen each beneficial owner for the legal entity against the OFAC list. You will go to [www.instanctofac.com](http://www.instanctofac.com) to complete this.

STEP 8: Complete the Customer Due Diligence Worksheet.

STEP 9: When you are completed with the account, please take the new account to the BSA/AML Officer to be reviewed.

**Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) for Higher Risk Customers**

Certain entities (businesses), products, services, individuals, and geographic areas are more likely to be involved with money laundering, terrorist financing, and other illegal activities. The Bank is to review its products, operations, and customer base in an effort to identify any high-risk areas.

Enhanced due diligence should be performed prior to account opening and reviewed at least annually during the account relationship.

**Higher Risk Customers and Entities**

* Foreign financial institutions, including banks and foreign money service providers (e.g., casas de Cambio, currency exchangers, money transmitters)  
    
* Non-bank financial institutions (e.g., money services businesses, casinos and card clubs; brokers/dealers in securities; and dealers in precious metals, stones, and jewels).  
    
* Senior foreign political figures and their immediate family members and close associates (PEPs) (known as politically exposed persons).  
    
* Nonresident alien (NRA) and accounts of foreign individuals.  
    
* Foreign corporations and domestic business entities, particularly offshore corporations (domestic shell companies and Private Investment Companies and international business corporations located in high-risk geographic locations.  
    
* Deposit brokers  
    
* Cash-intensive businesses (e.g., convenience stores, restaurants, retail stores, liquor stores, cigarette distributors, privately owned ATMs, vending machine operators, and parking garages).  
    
* Non-governmental organizations and charities (foreign and domestic).  
    
* Professional service providers (attorneys, accountants, doctors, or real estate brokers).

**Higher Risk Geographic Locations**

Higher-risk geographic locations can be either international or domestic. 

International higher-risk geographic locations generally include: 

* Countries subject to OFAC sanctions, including state sponsors of terrorism.

* Countries identified as supporting international terrorism under section 6(j) of the Export Administration Act of 1979, as determined by the Secretary of State.

* Jurisdictions determined to be “of primary money laundering concern” by the Secretary of the Treasury, and jurisdictions subject to special measures imposed by the Secretary of the Treasury, through FinCEN, pursuant to section 311 of the USA PATRIOT Act. 

* Jurisdictions or countries monitored for deficiencies in their regimes to combat money laundering and terrorist financing by international entities such as the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

* Major money laundering countries and jurisdictions identified in the U.S. Department of State’s annual International Narcotics Control Strategy Report (INCSR), in particular, countries which are identified as jurisdictions of primary concern.

* Offshore financial centers (OFC). 

* Other countries identified by the bank as higher-risk because of its prior experiences or other factors (e.g., legal considerations, or allegations of official corruption). 

Domestic higher-risk geographic locations may include, but are not limited to, banking offices doing business within, or having customers located within, a U.S. government-designated higher-risk geographic location. 

Domestic higher-risk geographic locations include:

* High Intensity Drug Trafficking Areas (HIDTA)  
    
* High Intensity Financial Crime Areas (HIFCA)

**Higher Risk Products and Services**

* Electronic funds payment services — electronic cash (e.g., prepaid and payroll cards), funds transfers (domestic and international), payable upon proper identification (PUPID) transactions, third-party payment processors, remittance activity, automated clearing house (ACH) transactions, and automated teller machines (ATM). 

* Electronic banking 

* Monetary instruments –cashier’s checks

* Services provided to third party payment processors or senders. 

* Lending activities, particularly loans secured by cash collateral and marketable securities. 

* Non-deposit account services (e.g., non-deposit investment products and insurance).

**EDD Procedures for Higher Risk Accounts**

**Persons and Entities**

**Nonresident Aliens (NRA) and Foreign Individuals** – Foreign individuals maintaining relationships with U.S. banks can be divided into two categories: resident aliens and nonresident aliens. An NRA is a non-U.S. citizen who: is not a lawful permanent resident of the United States during the calendar year and who does not meet the substantial presence test, [^24] or has not been issued an alien registration receipt card (green card).  

* Obtain Senior Management approval for establishing a relationship with such customers  
    
* Apply the Bank’s CIP   
    
* Consider the home country of the individual  
    
* Assess the types of products and services requested  
    
* Source of funds and anticipated account activity  
    
* Complete a W8 if necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship

**Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs)** – Generally includes a current or former senior foreign political figure, their immediate family, and their close associates.  
“Senior Political Figure” is a senior official in the executive, legislative, administrative, military or judicial branches of a foreign government (whether elected or not), a senior official of a major foreign political party, or a senior executive of a foreign government-owned corporation.  In addition, a senior political figure includes any corporation, business, or other entity that has been formed by, or for the benefit of, a senior foreign political figure.

“Immediate Family” typically includes the figure’s parents, siblings, spouse, children and in-laws.

“Close Associate” is a person who is widely and publicly known to maintain an unusually close relationship with the figure and includes a person who is in a position to conduct substantial domestic and international financial transactions on behalf of the senior foreign political figure.

* Determine whether the person’s status is “PEP” by asking about the official responsibilities of their office, if their title is honorary or salaried, level and nature of authority or influence over government activities or other officials, and access to access to significant government assets or funds.  
    
* Obtain senior management approval for establishing relationships with such customers  
    
* Apply the bank’s CIP  
    
* Identify countries of residence and the level of risk for corruption and money laundering associated with these locations.  
    
* Determine source of wealth and funds  
    
* Determine the purpose of the account and the expected volume and nature of account activity.  
    
* Conduct a basic risk assessment to determine level of risk and if further due diligence is necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary

**Business Entities –** A **“CIP Business Profile” and “Business Risk Assessment & Due Diligence”** will be completed for all new business accounts and maintained with the account documents.  

**Non- Bank Financial Institutions** \- (NBFI) provide financial services such as check cashing, currency exchanges, check sales, stored value card sales, or monetary transmissions for its customers. (e.g., casinos and card clubs, securities and commodities firms, money services businesses, insurance companies, dealers in precious metals, stones, or jewels, pawnbrokers, loan or finance companies)

A “Non-Bank Financial Institution” (NBFI) detail form is required to be completed and signed by the customer.  

**Money Services Business** \- (MSB) is an NBFI that conducts more than $1000 in business with one person in one or more transactions (in any category listed below) on the same day in one or more of the following services:

* Check cashing  
    
* Currency dealing or exchange or  
    
* Provides money transfer services in any amount.

MSBs:

* Complete the “NBFI Detail form including the MSB portion  
    
* Apply the bank’s CIP  
    
* Confirm FinCEN registration, if required  
    
* Confirm compliance with state or local licensing requirements, if applicable  
    
* Confirm agent status, if applicable  
    
* Conduct a basic risk assessment to determine level of risk and if further due diligence is necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary

**Professional Service Providers –** act as an intermediary between its client and the bank. This group includes lawyers, accountants, investment brokers, and other third parties that act as financial liaisons for their clients. These providers may conduct financial dealings for their clients. For example, an attorney may perform services for a client, or arrange for services to be performed on the client’s behalf, such as settlement of real estate transactions, asset transfers, management of client monies, investment services, and trust arrangements. 

A typical example is interest on lawyers’ trust accounts (IOLTA). These accounts contain funds for a lawyer’s various clients, and act as a standard bank account with one unique feature: The interest earned on the account is ceded to the state bar association or another entity for public interest and pro bono purposes. 

In contrast to escrow accounts that are set up to serve individual clients, professional service provider accounts allow for ongoing business transactions with multiple clients. Generally, a bank has no direct relationship with or knowledge of the beneficial owners of these accounts, who may be a constantly changing group of individuals and legal entities. 

* Apply the Bank’s CIP   
    
* Intended use of the account  
    
* Consider the geographic locations involved in the relationship  
    
* Assess the types of products and services requested  
    
* Source of funds and anticipated transaction volume  
    
* Conduct a basic risk assessment to determine level of risk and if further due diligence is necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary

**Nongovernmental Organizations and Charities –** are private nonprofit organizations that pursue activities intended to serve the public good. They can be any nonprofit organization that is independent from government. 

NGOs can range from large regional, national, or international charities to community-based self-help groups. This group also includes research institutes, churches, professional associations, and lobby groups that typically depend, in whole or in part, on charitable donations and voluntary service for support. 

*  Apply the Bank’s CIP   
    
* Obtain the purpose and objectives of their stated activities, organizational structure, and donor and volunteer base  
    
* What geographic locations are served (including headquarters and operational areas)  
    
* Funding and disbursement criteria (including basic beneficiary information)  
    
* Conduct a basic risk assessment to determine level of risk and if further due diligence is necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary


**Cash Intensive Businesses –** These businesses and entities cover various industry sectors.  Common examples include, but are not limited to; convenience stores, restaurants, retail stores, liquor stores, cigarette distributors, privately owned automated teller machines (ATM), vending machine operators, or parking garages. 

* Apply the Bank’s CIP  
    
* Understand the customer’s business operations   
    
* Intended use of the account  
    
* Consider the geographic locations involved in the relationship  
    
* Assess the types of products and services requested  
    
* Source of funds and anticipated transaction volume  
    
* Conduct a basic risk assessment to determine level of risk and if further due diligence is necessary  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary

**Products and Services**

**Brokered Deposits \-** The use of brokered deposits is a common funding source for many banks. Recent technology developments allow brokers to provide bankers with increased access to a broad range of potential investors who have no relationship with the bank. Deposit brokers provide intermediary services for banks and investors. 

* Use only brokers approved by the Board of Directors in the “Committees, Officers, & Authorizations” (COA) document if the deposit is about 15% of total deposit or 25% total assets.  
    
* Apply the bank’s CIP to the broker that opens the account[^25]  
    
* Determine and confirm whether the deposit broker is a legitimate business in all operating locations where the business is conducted  
    
* Review the deposit broker’s business strategies, including targeted customer markets  
    
* Determine whether the deposit broker is subject to regulatory oversight.  
    
* Evaluate whether the deposit broker’s BSA/AML and OFAC policies, procedures, and processes are adequate – ascertain whether they perform sufficient Customer Due Diligence (CDD) including CIP procedures.  
    
* Determine whether the deposit broker screens clients for OFAC matches.  
    
* Evaluate the adequacy of the deposit broker’s BSA/AML and OFAC audits and ensure that they address compliance with applicable regulations and requirements.  
    
* Confirm broker registration   
    
* Enter into a signed contract between the deposit broker and the bank  
    
* Complete a profile and risk assessment  
    
* Maintain maximum purchase limits  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring of the relationship if necessary

**Privately Owned Automated Teller Machines \-** Privately owned ATMs are particularly susceptible to money laundering and fraud.  Operators of these ATMs are often included within the definition of an Independent Sales Organization (ISO).[^26]  They are typically found in convenience stores, bars, restaurants, grocery stores or check cashing establishments.  Some ISOs are large scale operators, but many privately-owned ATMs are owned by the proprietors of the establishments in which they are located.

ISOs link their ATMs to an ATM transaction network.  The network routes data to the customer’s bank to debit the customer’s account and then credits the ISO’s account, which could be located at a bank located anywhere in the world.  Payments are typically made through the ACH system.

* Review corporate documentation, licenses, permits, contracts, or references  
    
* Review public databases to determine the existence of issues with the provider  
    
* Document the controls of the currency service arrangements and determine whether legitimate currency generation is sufficient to services the machines  
    
* Obtain the addresses of all ATM locations, ascertain the types of businesses in which the ATMs are located, and identify targeted demographics.   
    
* Determine expected ATM activity levels, including currency withdrawals  
    
* Ascertain the sources of currency for the ATMs by reviewing copies of armored car contracts, lending arrangements, or any other documentation, as appropriate.  
    
* Conduct enhanced monitoring 




**Automated Clearing House Transactions (ACH) \-** an ACH transaction is a batch-processed, value-dated, electronic funds transfer between an originating and a receiving bank. An ACH credit transaction is originated by the accountholder sending funds (payer), while an ACH debit transaction is originated by the accountholder receiving funds (payee). Within the ACH system, these participants and users are known by the following terms: 

* Originator \- An organization or person that initiates an ACH transaction to an account either as a debit or credit

* Originating Depository Financial Institution (ODFI) \- The Originator’s depository financial institution that forwards the ACH transaction into the national ACH network through an ACH Operator

* ACH Operator \- processes all ACH transactions that flow between different depository financial institutions. An ACH Operator serves as a central clearing facility that receives entries from the ODFIs and distributes the entries to the appropriate Receiving Depository Financial Institution. There are currently two ACH Operators: FedACH and Electronic Payments Network (EPN)

* Receiving Depository Financial Institution (RDFI) \-The Receiver’s depository institution that receives the ACH transaction from the ACH Operators and credits or debits funds from their receivers’ accounts

* Receiver \- An organization or person that authorizes the Originator to initiate an ACH transaction, either as a debit or credit to an account

* Gateway Operator (GO) \- A financial institution, ACH Operator, or ODFI that acts as an entry or exit point to or from the United States. A formal declaration of status as a Gateway Operator is not required. ACH operators and ODFIs acting in the role of Gateway Operators have specific warranties and obligations related to certain international entries. A financial institution acting as a Gateway Operator generally may process inbound and outbound debit and credit transactions. ACH Operators acting as Gateway Operators may process outbound debit and credit entries, but can limit inbound entries to credit entries only and reversals. 

International ACH Payments (IAT) \- an ACH entry that is part of a payment transaction involving a financial agency’s office that is not located in the territorial jurisdiction of the United States. An office of a financial agency is involved in the payment transaction if one or more of the following conditions are met: 

Holds an account that is credited or debited as part of a payment transaction; or receives funds directly from a person or makes payment directly to a person as part of a payment transaction; or serves as an intermediary in the settlement of any part of a payment transaction. 

An “inbound entry” originates in another country and is transmitted to the United States. For example, an inbound entry could be funding for a company payroll. Each subsequent IAT used for direct deposit would be an inbound IAT entry. 

An “outbound entry” originates in the United States and is transmitted to another country. For example, IAT pension payments going from a U.S. ODFI to a U.S. RDFI in which the funds are then transferred to an account in another country would be outbound IAT entries. 

Refer to the “ACH Management Policy” for further information.

* Document the nature of business/type of company  
    
* Review the customer’s transaction types, volume, and third-party payment processor relationships if applicable  
    
* Purpose for and frequency of the ACHs  
    
* Total file daily transfer limit  
    
* Signed contract   
    
* Annual review of account and customer profile

**Monitoring**

Accounts will be monitored using the exception reports, and any other means deemed necessary by the BSA Compliance Officer. 

High risk rated accounts will be reviewed monthly.  Moderate risk accounts will be reviewed annually.  The BSA Compliance Officer will document the review and identify the following:

* Why the customer was initially determined to be of high risk.  
    
* Verify that information obtained is still current.  This will include purpose of account, physical location, source/use of funds, account relationships, and a review of the customer’s account history.

A file will be maintained containing the review, any identified unusual activity and the results of those investigations, and any risk rating changes.  
   
Once an account is opened, all employees who have contact with customers have accountability to monitor the account to ensure transactions are commensurate with normal activity associated with the account. 

**Suspicious Activity Reporting**

All employees during their normal course of business are required to report suspicious activity to the BSA compliance officer.

The bank offers the following products and services:

* Electronic Banking   
    
* Internet banking including: bill payment, statement retrieval, transfer funds within the bank  
    
* ATM & Point of Sale  
    
* Telephone banking  
    
* Funds Transfers   
    
* Wires-Incoming/Outgoing  
    
* Automatic Clearing House Transactions  
    
* Originator  
    
* ODFI & RDFI  
    
* IAT – Gateway Operator  
    
* Purchase and Sale of Monetary Instruments  
    
* Cashier’s checks  
    
* Brokered Deposits  
    
* Lending Activities  
    
* Commercial  
    
* Real Estate  
    
* Consumer   
    
* Revolving    
* Deposit Accounts   
    
* Checking  
    
* Star Checking  
    
* Star Plus Checking  
    
* Silver Star Now   
    
* Gold Star Now  
    
* Platinum Star  
* Small Business   
    
* Business Partner Analysis Account  
    
* Business Now   
    
* Business Money Market  
    
* Savings (regular)  
    
* Time Deposits (regular & IRA)   
    
    
    
* Safe Deposit Boxes

The bank does not currently offer the following products and services:  

* Foreign Correspondent Accounts  
    
* Bulk Shipments of Currency   
    
* U.S. Dollar Drafts  
    
* Payable Through Accounts  
    
* Pouch Activities  
    
* Electronic Cash  
    
* Third –Party Payment Processors  
    
* Non-deposit Investment Products  
    
* Insurance  
    
* Concentration Accounts  
    
* Trade Finance Activities  
    
* Private Banking  
    
* Trust and Asset Management Services

**Refer to the “Products and Services Analysis” for further information**

**Revision Tracking………………………………………………………. Risk Matrix**

The following matrix is designed to provide an adequate audit trail of revisions and maintenance to the BSA/AML/CIP Program with approval dates of required authorities.

Updated the policy. Added the 5th pillar Beneficial Ownership.		04-23-2018  
	  


[^1]:  Financial Crimes Enforcement Network, a bureau within the Department of the Treasury

[^2]:  Currency- coin and paper money of the United States or any other country, which is circulated and customarily used and accepted as money

[^3]:  Business Day- whatever the bank normally communicates to its depository customers regarding the routine posting of a transaction

[^4]:  Bank – combination of all offices

[^5]:  Knowledge – when a bank employee or system is aware of multiple transactions

[^6]:  Person – an individual or entity (corporation, partnership, trust, estate, association, organization, etc.)

[^7]:  Like – cash debit (cash –in) or cash credit (cash-out)

[^8]:  FinCEN advisory \#10 – October 1998 explains the reformed exemption process in detail.

[^9]:  Federal Reserve banks are not “banks” or government entities as defined by BSA.  Filing of a Designation of Exempt Person is not necessary.

[^10]:  Any subsidiary of any listed entity may be treated as an exempt person regardless of whether the subsidiary has adopted the corporate form of business. 

[^11]:  FinCEN directive – FIN-2009-G003 Guidance on Determining Eligibility for Exemption from Currency Transaction Reporting Requirements 8/31/2009

[^12]:  FinCEN’s *Guidance on Interpreting Financial Institution Policies in Relation to Recordkeeping Requirements under 31 CFR 103.29*  November 2002

[^13]:  Referred to as the “Travel Rule”

[^14]:  An established customer includes a person with a loan, deposit or other asset account or one for whom the institution has on file the person’s name, address, taxpayer identification number or passport number and country of issuance; and to which the institution provides financial services in reliance on that information.

[^15]:  A transaction includes a deposit or withdrawal; transfer between accounts; exchange of currency; extension of credit; a purchase or sale of any stock, bond, certificate of deposit, or monetary instrument or investment security; or any other payment, transfer, or delivery, by, through, or to the bank.

[^16]:  The term “customer” will include individuals, corporations, partnerships, trusts, and any signatory on an account

[^17]:  When an individual opens a new account for an entity that is not a legal person or for another individual who lacks legal capacity 

[^18]:  For an individual: a residential or business street address or if the individual does not have such an address, the bank may accept an APO or FPO box number, or the residential or business street address of next of kin or of another contact individual.  Bank records will indicate whose address is actually being used in such cases, and why.  

[^19]:  The principal place of business, local office address, or other physical location 

[^20]:  The customer identification number for a U.S. person shall be the taxpayer identification number.   

[^21]:  An “account” is defined as a formal banking relationship to provide or engage

[^22]:  The customer is not required to provide a formal document.  The bank will verify the information from the Secretary of State (SOS) web site.  This also applies to out of state corporations. Corporations doing continuous business in Kentucky must register with the SOS. If the entity is not in good standing, the account will not be opened.

[^23]:  Louisa Community Bank does not open business accounts for non-U.S. entities

[^24]:   A foreign national is a resident alien if the individual is physically present in the United States for at least 31 days in the current calendar year and present 183 days or more based on counting: all days present during the current year, plus one-third of the days present in the preceding year, plus one-sixth of the days present in the second preceding year. Certain days of presence are disregarded, such as (i) days spent in the United States for a medical condition that developed while the foreign national was present in the United States and unable to leave, (ii) days regular commuters spend traveling to or from Canada or Mexico, (iii) a day of less than 24 hours spent while in transit between two locations outside the United States., and (iv) days when the foreign national was an exempt individual. The individual is considered a resident alien for federal income and employment tax purposes from the first day of physical presence in the United States in the year that the test is satisfied. 

[^25]:  For the purpose of the CIP rule, in the case of brokered deposits, the “customer” will be the broker that opens the account.  A bank will not need to look through the deposit broker’s account to determine the identity of each individual sub-accountholder, if need only verify the identity of the named accountholder. FFIEC BSA/AML Examination Manual 11/17/2014

[^26]:  An ISO typically acts as an agent for merchants, including ATM owners, to process electronic transactions.  In some cases, the ATM owner may act as its own ISO processor.

---

### Source: BSA_AML_CIP_OFAC Program Policy.doc.md

**Organizational Functional Area:	Compliance**

**Policy For:	BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC Program**  
	  
**Board Approved:	09/25/2014**  
**Last Revision Date:	09/26/2013**

**Individual Responsible**  
**for Maintaining/Updating Policy:	Compliance/BSA Officer**

### 

**BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC Program**

**PURPOSE**

The **Bank Secrecy Act (BSA)** was designed to fight drug trafficking, money laundering, terrorist financing, and other illicit financial transactions.  Congress enacted the BSA to help prevent banks and other financial service providers from being used as intermediaries for, or being used to hide the transfer or deposit of money derived from, criminal activity.  Suspicious activity reporting forms the cornerstone of the BSA reporting system and is incorporated into a financial institution’s **Anti-Money Laundering (AML)** program.  The passage of the USA PATRIOT Act after the terrorist attacks required that banks have a written **Customer Identification Program (CIP)** that is incorporated into the BSA/AML program.  The CIP is intended to enable the bank to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer.  Finally, the **Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)** administers and enforces economic and trade sanctions based on U.S. foreign policy and national security goals against entities such as targeted foreign countries, terrorists, international narcotics traffickers, and those engaged in activities related to the proliferations of weapons of mass destruction.

Be it resolved that this is the policy of Bank \_\_\_ (Bank \_\_\_ or Bank) to maintain maximum compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act (BSA), Anti-Money Laundering (AML) efforts, the Customer Identification Program (CIP) rules, and the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) requirements under Federal, FDIC, and Internal Revenue Service regulations.  This is referred to as the “BSA program.” 

**BSA COMPLIANCE PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS**

The Federal banking agencies have adopted rules that require each bank to develop and provide for the continued administration of a compliance program that, at a minimum, provides for:

1. designation of an individual to be responsible for the coordination and monitoring of daily compliance.  
2. periodic training to all appropriate personnel, which will be tailored to the person’s specific responsibilities for the BSA.  
3. independent testing for compliance concerning the BSA program every 12 to 18 months by qualified external auditors.  
4. a system of internal controls to assure ongoing compliance with the requirements of BSA, detecting and preventing money laundering, and reporting suspicious activities.

**COMPLIANCE RESPONSIBILITIES**

The Board of Directors will be ultimately responsible for the financial institution’s BSA compliance (certain responsibilities may be delegated to the Compliance Committee), as follows:

* Reviewing and approving the written BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC compliance program at least annually.  
* Understanding the importance of BSA regulatory requirements, ramifications of noncompliance, and risks posed to the Bank.  
* Appointing a qualified individual as the BSA Officer.  
* Ensuring that the BSA Officer has sufficient authority and resources (monetary, physical and personnel) to administer an effective risk-based BSA compliance program.  
* Reviewing audits and training programs as needed.  
* Reviewing recommendations regarding the BSA compliance program made by the BSA Officer, external auditors, or examiners.  
* Maintaining awareness of the Bank’s BSA program efforts through review of periodic reports by the BSA Officer.  
* Keeping BSA/AML matters discussed at Board meetings confidential such as suspicious activity reporting.

The Board of Directors has designated \_\_\_ as the BSA Officer who is responsible for coordinating and monitoring day-to-day compliance and managing all aspects of the BSA and OFAC programs. The BSA Officer (or other designated employees) will be responsible for the following concerning compliance with the BSA program (not all-inclusive list):

* Developing and maintaining BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC policies and procedures.  
* Monitoring, supervising, coordinating, and documenting the BSA program, including the timely filing of all required BSA reports; making recommendations when and where appropriate to enhance the BSA program.  
* Maintaining proficient knowledge of the regulations and guidance, including but not limited to, the BSA, USA Patriot Act, and OFAC.  
* Researching regulatory issues and responding to BSA-related compliance questions from Bank personnel.  
* Disseminating BSA-related information to appropriate staff and providing assistance where necessary.  
* Providing periodic reports to the Board of Directors regarding BSA activities, including suspicious activity reporting.  
* Reviewing OFAC and Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) reports, including 314(a) requests, and assuring compliance with current regulations.  
* Monitoring the Currency Transaction Reporting (CTR) system and ensuring timely filing of accurate reports.  
* Reviewing various monetary instrument, wire transfer, large transaction, kiting suspect, and similar reports for any type of suspicious activity.  
* Monitoring the customer base for suspicious activity and providing Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) for a second review and to the Board and appropriate regulatory agencies.  
* Ensuring compliance with the Customer Identification Program (CIP) requirements and internal procedures.  
* Conducting a risk assessment of each customer and monitoring high-risk and moderate-risk customers with documented reviews of activity levels.  
* Performing a risk assessment of the Bank’s customer base, products and services, geographic location, and operations every 12 to 18 months; performing a similar OFAC risk assessment.  
* Providing and/or coordinating periodic training to existing and new employees and Board members.  
* Reviewing any additions or deletions from the BSA exemption list and completing reports in this area where necessary.  
* Reviewing and implementing, where feasible, any anti-money laundering software.  
* Serving as liaison between the Bank, the FDIC, the State, and external auditors for examinations and audits.    
* Providing all BSA examination and audit reports to the Compliance Committee, including  responses and corrective actions.  
* Maintaining records of all BSA reporting for a five-year period.

**BSA TRAINING**

A training program will be in effect to assure all applicable personnel are properly trained in BSA responsibilities appropriate to their area.  The BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC Employee Manual (also referred to as BSA Employee Manual) will be utilized during training, including orientation of new employees, where applicable.  In addition, the BSA Officer and/or BSA Specialist will provide training to employees and Board members on a periodic basis.  On-line BSA-related training also may be required by certain employees, including new employees and refresher courses for existing employees.  The BSA Officer, with the assistance of Human Resources, will maintain a record of individuals trained, dates of training, and a copy of any materials presented.  The BSA Officer, and any designated BSA staff, shall periodically attend educational programs related to BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC compliance.

**INDEPENDENT TESTING**

The Bank will approve a qualified external auditor to be responsible for conducting an independent risk-based evaluation for compliance with the BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC requirements every 12 to 18 months.  The audit should be done utilizing risk-based procedures that cover all of the Bank’s activities.  At a minimum, testing should be performed using the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council (FFIEC) BSA/AML Examination Manual guidelines.  A written report of the audit results and steps taken to remedy any deficiencies shall be reported to the Compliance Committee of the Board.

In addition, the BSA Officer may perform internal reviews of various BSA-related areas to determine if staff are adhering to the procedures contained in the BSA Employee Manual and BSA Operations Procedures Manual.  Any results of these BSA reviews will be provided to the Compliance Committee. All audit/examination/internal review findings and recommendations will be tracked on a spreadsheet to ensure an effective system is in place for correction of items.

**INTERNAL CONTROLS**

The Bank shall institute a system of internal controls to ensure ongoing compliance that includes, but is not limited to:

* Performing a BSA and OFAC risk assessment by identifying the financial institution’s operations (products, services, customers, and geographic locations) more vulnerable to abuse by money launderers and criminals; providing for periodic updates to the Bank’s risk profile (generally every 12 – 18 months); and providing for a BSA/AML compliance program tailored to manage risks.  
* Incorporating a review of BSA/OFAC risks, when necessary, for any new products or services introduced by the Bank.  
* Informing the Board of Directors and senior management of BSA activities every other month that incorporates the following (subject to change):  
  * Suspicious Activity Report(s) – brief description (if insider transaction, Board will be notified at next scheduled monthly meeting or sooner)  
  * Do Not File Report – brief description of activity not considered SAR reportable  
  * Currency Transaction Reports – number filed during review period  
  * Foreign Wires – number and dollar amount   
  * High Risk Accounts – number reported  
  * BSA Training – brief summary of training  
  * BSA Audits – brief summary of any recent BSA-related audits  
  * BSA Regulatory Issues – description of proposed, new or revised regulations or other guidance from regulatory agencies  
  * Other BSA Issues – if needed, will be described  
* Providing for program continuity despite changes in management or employee composition or structure.  
* Meeting all regulatory recordkeeping and reporting requirements, meeting recommendations for BSA/AML compliance, and providing for timely updates in response to changes in regulations.  
* Implementing risk-based customer due diligence (CDD) policies, procedures, and processes.  
* Providing sufficient controls, reviews, separation of duties, and monitoring systems for timely detection and reporting of suspicious activity and accurately filing all required reports including SARs, CTRs, and CTR exemptions.  
* Providing for adequate supervision of employees that handle currency transactions, monitoring CIP procedural requirements, completing reports, granting exemptions, monitoring for suspicious activity, or engaging in any other activity covered by the BSA and its implementing regulations.

**BSA AND OFAC REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS**

The Bank recognizes BSA and OFAC compliance is a safety and soundness issue with several key requirements.  In accordance with these requirements, it is the policy of the Bank to:

* Report currency transactions exceeding $10,000 to the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN).  
* Grant exemptions to currency transaction reporting, where appropriate.  
* Obtain certain information from customers and non-customers who purchase monetary instruments for cash in amounts from $3,000 to $10,000.  
* Collect and retain certain information in connection with funds transfers of $3,000 or more.  
* Retain certain records for a period of five years.  
* Establish a risk-based Customer Identification Program.  
* Institute a formal risk-based Anti-Money Laundering Program.  
* Report suspicious activities to the Board of Directors and appropriate authorities.  
* Review government lists.  
* Communicate with third parties through information sharing covered under the 314(a) and 314(b) regulatory requirements.  
* Ensure accountholders and certain types of transactions are verified for OFAC compliance and block or prohibit transactions when required per risk-based procedures.  
* Report all blockings to OFAC when required.

Specific procedures regarding compliance with the aforementioned BSA and OFAC requirements are separately outlined in the written BSA/AML/CIP/OFAC Compliance Program Employee Manual and the BSA Operations Procedures Manual.  Employees shall be aware of their responsibilities and accountability for compliance with all BSA and OFAC-related requirements, which are described in the two aforementioned manuals.

**ANTI-MONEY LAUNDERING PROGRAM**

The objectives of the Anti-Money Laundering program are to:

* Facilitate the Bank’s compliance with the BSA and other related laws and regulations, including filing SARs as required.  
* Ensure full compliance with the Bank’s established Customer Identification Program.  
* Ensure employees are knowledgeable regarding the money laundering process and suspicious transactions.  
* Facilitate safe and sound banking practices.  
* Decrease the likelihood that the institution will become a victim of illegal activities perpetrated by its customers.  
* Protect the good name and reputation of BOCF.

It is the policy of the Bank \_\_\_ to implement policies and procedures that assist in detecting and preventing money laundering, terrorist financing, or other illegal activities conducted through transactions at the Bank.   In accordance with Section 352 of the USA PATRIOT Act and 31 CFR 1020.210, BOCF has established formal procedures to minimize exposure to transactions, compliance, and reputation risks associated with money laundering. The Bank makes risk-based assessments to identify potential risks associated with products and services offered, the customers served, the Bank’s operations, and the geographic locations of the offices.  To manage risk, the AML procedures include:

* Account opening controls in accordance with the Customer Identification Program.  
* Customer due diligence (CDD) and enhanced due diligence (EDD) efforts.  
* Screening of government lists, when applicable.  
* Daily, weekly, and monthly reviews of various reports such as CTR large transaction reports, kiting suspect reports, monetary instrument logs, wire logs, automated clearinghouse (ACH) reports, and suspicious tracking reports.  
* Monitoring and reporting of suspicious activity.

All employees shall be provided information as to the types of suspicious activity that could occur within their operational positions.  Procedures have been developed to report suspicious activity, when warranted, to the BSA Officer (or designated staff).  A decision will be made by the BSA Department as to whether a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) will be filed; the SAR is prepared as necessary by the BSA Officer (or designated staff); forwarded for confirmation through a second review process; sent in a timely manner to the appropriate Federal agency and state and local authorities (if applicable); and reported to the Board at the next scheduled BSA Activity report (insider SAR transactions to be reported at next scheduled Board meeting if not sooner).  If a SAR is not required, files will be maintained for Do Not File (DNF) activity. A BSA Committee structure is in place to make a determination on whether to keep open or close accounts for subjects who have generally had three consecutive SARs filed.  All Board members and employees are trained on the importance of maintaining the confidentiality of SAR information.  Specific SAR-related procedures are described in the BSA Employee Manual and the BSA Operations Procedures Manual.

**CUSTOMER IDENTIFICATION PROGRAM**

It is the policy of the Bank \_\_\_ to form a reasonable belief that it knows the true identity of each customer through risk-based procedures.  As required by Section 326 of the USA PATRIOT Act and 31 CFR 1020.220, the BOCF has implemented a Customer Identification Program, consisting of procedures for:

* Verifying the identity of any customer opening an account, to the extent reasonable and practicable.  
* Maintaining records of the information used to verify the customer’s identity, including name, address, date of birth (for individuals), and taxpayer identification number for U.S. persons and number and country of issuance of a government-issued document for non-U.S. persons.  
* Determining if the customer appears on any government list of known or suspected terrorists or terrorist organizations for purposes of Section 326 provided to the Bank by any government agency.   
* Providing adequate customer notice that the Bank is requesting information to verify their identities.  
* Determining whether a SAR should be filed (see separate Anti-Money Laundering section of this policy).

In compliance with the CIP rules, the Bank will take appropriate steps to ensure the accuracy of the customer information collected.  The Bank uses documentary verification (e.g., driver’s license) and non-documentary verification (e.g., credit report) methods to verify customer information. In addition, risk-based procedures have been established for customer due diligence (CDD) and enhanced due diligence (EDD) to differentiate between lower risk and higher risk customers.  

Due to the high risk nature of the entity, it is the policy of the Bank not to open accounts for the following:

* Politically Exposed Person (PEP) – includes a current or former senior foreign political figure, their immediate family, and any close associates.  
* Money Services Business (MSB) – includes currency dealers or exchangers; check cashers; issuers of traveler’s checks, money orders, or prepaid access cards; sellers or redeemers of traveler’s checks, money orders, or prepaid access cards; or money transmitters that meet the threshold requirement of transactions in an amount greater than $1,000 for any person on any day in one or more transactions (money transmitters do not have this threshold and are considered a MSB).  
* Unlawful Internet Gambling Accounts – includes commercial customers that may use an account for unlawful gambling activities under federal, state, and Regulation GG laws.  
* Payment Processor Relationships – includes deposit customers who may process payments for merchant clients (certain merchants, such as telemarketing and online, may pose a greater money laundering and fraud risk).  
* Privately Owned ATM Entities – includes businesses who may have privately owned ATMs in locations such as convenience stores, bars, restaurants, or check cashing establishments.  
* Marijuana\-Related Business – includes businesses who deal with the production, distribution or sale of either medical marijuana or recreational marijuana.  
* Virtual Currency Entity – includes businesses who serves as an administrator or exchanger that accepts and transmits convertible virual currency or buys or sells convertible virtual currency.

In addition, the Bank will not generally accept deposits through “deposit brokers.”  Use of deposit brokers can present additional safety and soundness and BSA/AML/OFAC risk to the Bank.  Enhanced procedures have been instituted to identify and minimize opening the aforementioned high-risk accounts.

Complete details of internal CIP procedures are included in the BSA Employee Manual and the BSA Operations Procedures Manual.

**INFORMATION SHARING**

It is the policy of Bank \_\_\_ to comply with all provisions regarding the USA PATRIOT Act’s Section 314(a) process required by FinCEN.  This process involves law enforcement agencies funneling requests through FinCEN, who in turn communicate with financial institutions to determine whether individuals and entities suspected of engaging in money laundering or terrorist financing activities are accountholders of the financial institution upon a records search.  The BSA Officer, or designated employee, will be responsible for searching records within 14 calendar days of each 314(a) request and reporting any positive matches.

It is the policy of Bank \_\_\_ to comply with regulations involving Section 314(b) of the USA PATRIOT Act, when applicable.  The regulation establishes a mechanism where financial institutions are allowed to register to share information with other financial institutions in order to identify and report activities that they believe may relate to terrorist activity or money laundering.  The Bank has registered with FinCEN and is allowed to share information with other institutions who also are registered.  In order to continue with Section 314(b) information sharing, the Bank will be required to renew the certification annually.

More comprehensive procedures are referenced in the BSA Operations Procedures Manual.

**OFFICE OF FOREIGN ASSETS CONTROL (OFAC) PROGRAM**

In general, OFAC regulations:

* Require the blocking or freezing of accounts and other assets of specified countries, entities, and individuals.  
* Prohibit unlicensed trade and financial transactions with specified countries, entities, and individuals. 

The OFAC list (Specially Designated Nationals) is updated frequently and readily available to financial institutions.  New accounts will be compared with the OFAC list prior to being opened or shortly thereafter, and wire transactions will be screened for OFAC compliance prior to transmission.  In addition, risk-based procedures will be established to review certain types of transactions (e.g., cashing on-us checks) against the OFAC list. 

It is the policy of Bank \_\_\_ to comply with all Federal laws, regulations, and orders regarding doing business with, maintaining accounts for, or handling transactions or monetary transfers for individuals or entities on the OFAC list and targeted countries.  If it is found that the Bank has an account or a customer of the Bank is shown on the OFAC list, all accounts of such customer shall be blocked and held in accordance with instructions from the U.S. Treasury Department.

To comply with OFAC requirements, the Bank has made risk-based assessments and implemented procedures consisting of:

* Maintaining the list of prohibited countries, entities, and individuals (OFAC list).  
* Comparing new accounts  and certain types of transactions to the OFAC list.  
* Comparing incoming and outgoing funds transfers to the OFAC list.  
* Reviewing international automated clearinghouse transactions (IATs) to the OFAC list.  
* Performing an OFAC review of new employees and new vendors for accounts payable transactions.  
* Monitoring transactions for possible prohibited activity.  
* Comparing the Bank’s entire customer database to the OFAC list on a monthly basis.  
* Having a process for determining valid matches versus false hits.  
* Having a process for blocking accounts and rejecting transactions, where applicable, and for notifying OFAC and the customer.

Specific procedures are detailed separately in the BSA Employee Manual and the BSA Operations Manual.

**PENALTIES**

Community banks are at a greater risk today of being used by money launderers and terrorists, as larger financial institutions and those in locations more likely to be used by money launderers become more vigilant.  Should terrorists or money launderers use a community bank, the consequences could be severe and include the following:

* Bank employees and banks can be fined and employees imprisoned.  
* Any property involved or traceable to the proceeds of criminal activity, including loan collateral and bank accounts, may be subject to forfeiture.  
* Bank may risk losing charter.  
* Bank employee termination of employment.  
* Bank employee may be removed and barred from banking.  
* Potential supervisory enforcement action by regulatory agency(ies), including civil money penalties and cease-and-desist orders.  
* Bad media press and decline in public confidence.  
* Limitations on bank applications such as expansion of branches or acquisitions.

It is important to note that criminal and civil penalties can be imposed on Bank personnel or the institution for willfully and knowingly assisting in money laundering or structuring transactions to avoid CTR reporting.  A Bank employee could be subject to a criminal fine of up to $250,000 or five years in prison, or both.  A person who commits such a violation while violating another U.S. law, or engaging in a pattern of criminal activity, is subject to a fine of up to $500,000 or ten years in prison, or both.  A bank that violates certain BSA provisions faces criminal money penalties up to the greater of $1 million or twice the value of the transaction.

Other specific penalties include:

* Failure to maintain compliance procedures may cause the imposition of civil penalties of up to a maximum of $1,000 per day for each day of non-compliance.  *This includes procedures related to customer identification.*  
* A penalty of not more than $500 per violation can be imposed if the Bank violates the recordkeeping or reporting requirements of the BSA.  Willful violations may cause penalties in an amount equivalent to that of the transaction (up to $100,000) or $25,000, whichever is greater.  *This includes filing a CTR, maintaining monetary instrument logs, wire transfer logs, etc.*  
* If a required CTR is not filed timely, a $10,000 per day civil money penalty may be imposed until it is filed.

There are both civil and criminal penalties for OFAC violations.  Depending on the sanctions program, criminal penalties can include fines ranging from $50,000 to $10 million and imprisonment ranging from 10 to 30 years for willful violations.  Civil penalties range from $250,000 or twice the amount of each underlying transaction to $1,075,000 for each violation.  The majority of fines result from a bank’s failure to block illicit transfers (like wires) when there was a reference to a targeted country or Specially Designated Nationals (SDNs).  The Bank also could face the risk of the collateral for loan transactions being subject to forfeiture if the individual or entity is identified as being on the OFAC SDN list.

##### ***Revision Table***

| Description of change | Revision | Author | Date |
| :---- | :---: | :---- | :---- |
